1.1:
δοῦλος (doulos) — the standard English translation "servant" understates the Greek: a doulos is a slave, one who belongs entirely to the master. Paul places this self-designation first, before "apostle," making total ownership by Christ the ground of his commission rather than its limitation.

ἀφωρισμένος (aphorismenon) — "set apart," perfect passive participle. The same root as Pharisee (perushim, the separated ones). Paul the former Pharisee has been re-separated — not from Gentile contamination but for the gospel. The word signals a deliberate reorientation of his prior separatist vocation.
1.2: προεπηγγείλατο (proepēngeilato) — "promised beforehand," aorist middle of proepangellomai. The pro- prefix is emphatic: the gospel is not new but the fulfillment of prior divine commitment. The agent is God; the medium is "his prophets in the holy scriptures" — graphais hagiais, which identifies the OT as a single unified text of sacred promise.
1.3: κατὰ σάρκα (kata sarka) — "according to the flesh" — the preposition kata with the accusative denotes the sphere or standard of measurement. The Davidic descent is real and historical but belongs to the sphere of natural descent; v4 will supply the contrasting sphere.
1.4:
ὁρισθέντος (horisthentos) — aorist passive participle of horizō, "to mark off, designate, declare." The NKJV "declared" is preferable to "predestined" here; Paul is not speaking of an eternal decree but of the resurrection as a public declaration that Jesus is who he claimed to be. The aorist points to the single historical event of the resurrection as the moment of declaration.

ἐξ ἀναστάσεως νεκρῶν — "by resurrection from the dead" (genitive of means). The resurrection is not merely what happened to Jesus but the revelatory event that declared his identity with power (en dynamei). The power-phrase modifies the Son-of-God designation, not the resurrection itself: he was declared Son-of-God-in-power by means of the resurrection.
1.5: χάριν καὶ ἀποστολήν (charin kai apostolēn) — "grace and apostleship." The kai here may be explanatory ("grace, specifically apostleship") rather than additive — the apostolic commission is itself a form of grace. The goal: ὑπακοὴν πίστεως (hypakoēn pisteōs) — "obedience of faith," a genitive of apposition (the obedience that consists in faith) or of source (the obedience that flows from faith); both are true.
1.6: κλητοὶ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (klētoi Iēsou Christou) — "called of/belonging to Jesus Christ," genitive of possession. The letter's audience is defined by their relationship to Christ through divine calling, not by ethnicity or prior religious affiliation. The klētos (called) root recurs in v7 ("called to be saints") and forms an inclusio with v1 ("called to be an apostle").
1.7: κλητοῖς ἁγίοις (klētois hagiois) — "called saints" — not aspirationally holy but constitutively designated. The participle is a verbal adjective: they are saints because they are called, not called because they are already saintly. χάρις and εἰρήνη (charis kai eirēnē) — the Pauline greeting replaces the standard Greek chairein (greetings) with charis (grace), and adds the Hebrew shalom (peace) — a fusion of Greco-Roman and Jewish greeting forms that announces the letter's cross-cultural address.
1.8: ἡ πίστις ὑμῶν (hē pistis hymōn) — "your faith," with the definite article emphasizing the particular character of the Roman community's reputation. καταγγέλλεται (katangelletai) — present passive, "is being proclaimed/announced" — the gospel-proclamation verb used of the good news; the Roman church's faith is itself a form of proclamation throughout the known world.
1.9: λατρεύω (latreuō) — "serve as a priest" or "render cultic service." Paul describes his prayer life with the vocabulary of temple service, placing intercessory prayer within the framework of priestly ministry. ἐν τῷ πνεύματί μου — "in my spirit" — the locus of Paul's gospel service is interior, not in the external rites of the temple cult.
1.10: εὐοδωθήσομαι (euodōthēsomai) — "to be prospered/given a good road" (odos = road, eu- = good). The travel metaphor is embedded in the word itself. Paul frames his desire to visit Rome as a petition for God to give him the right road — a combination of circumstantial opportunity and divine guidance.
1.11: μεταδῶ χάρισμά τι πνευματικόν (metadō charisma ti pneumatikon) — "to share some spiritual gift." The indefinite ti ("some") is tactfully vague — Paul does not specify what gift he would bring, likely to avoid appearing presumptuous with a community he has not founded.
1.12: συμπαρακληθῆναι (symparaklēthēnai) — "to be mutually encouraged together" — the syn- prefix makes the encouragement reciprocal; Paul revises his v11 statement from one-directional giving to mutual benefit. The verb's root is paraklēsis (comfort/exhortation), showing that even the apostle expects to receive from the community rather than only imparting.
1.13: τινα καρπὸν σχῶ (tina karpon schō) — "that I might have some fruit" — the aorist subjunctive indicates Paul's goal-orientation. The fruit-metaphor for missionary results is consistent with Paul's agricultural imagery elsewhere (1 Cor 3:6-9; Col 1:6). καθὼς καὶ ἐν τοῖς λοιποῖς ἔθνεσιν — "as also among the rest of the Gentiles" — Paul includes Rome in his Gentile mission-field, perhaps a delicate claim given Rome's mixed Jewish-Gentile community.
1.14: Ἕλλησίν τε καὶ βαρβάροις (Hellēsin te kai barbarois) — "to Greeks and to barbarians." The Greek world divided all humanity into those who spoke Greek and those whose speech sounded like "bar-bar" (unintelligible noise). Paul's commission covers both cultural categories — the civilized and the uncivilized. σοφοῖς τε καὶ ἀνοήτοις — "to the wise and to the foolish" — a second axis covering intellectual status: educated and uneducated alike are Paul's debtors.
1.15: πρόθυμον (prothymon) — "eager/ready," from pro-thymos (forward-minded, with forward spirit). The eagerness Paul claims is not reluctant duty but genuine missionary desire. The present infinitive euangelisasthai indicates not a one-time act but an ongoing readiness to proclaim.
1.16: οὐ γὰρ ἐπαισχύνομαι (ou gar epaischunomai) — "for I am not ashamed" — the negated litotes (understatement) = Paul is positively proud of and committed to the gospel. In Roman honor-shame culture, the crucifixion was a symbol of shame; Paul's non-shame is a deliberate inversion. εἰς σωτηρίαν (eis sōtērian) — "unto salvation" — the purpose clause: power exercised for a goal, not power for display. Ἰουδαίῳ τε πρῶτον καὶ Ἕλληνι — "to the Jew first and also to the Greek" — the priority is historical-redemptive (Jew first = covenant priority) not chronologically mandatory for each city, as Paul's own practice varied.
1.17:
δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ (dikaiosynē theou) — the most debated genitive in Paul. Grammatically it can be: (a) God's attribute ("God's own righteousness"), (b) a righteousness God gives ("righteousness from God"), or (c) God's faithfulness to his covenant. Paul's usage across Romans suggests all three overlap: God's own righteousness is given as gift through the covenant faithfulness demonstrated in Christ.

ἐκ πίστεως εἰς πίστιν (ek pisteōs eis pistin) — "from faith to faith." The doubled construction has been read as: (a) from beginning faith to mature faith (progressive), (b) from the faithfulness of God/Christ to the faith of the believer (movement from source to recipient), or (c) from faith alone to faith alone (emphatic). The phrase resists reduction to a single interpretation; Paul may be intentionally layered.
1.18:
ἀποκαλύπτεται (apokalypto) — present passive indicative, "is being revealed" — the same verb used of the gospel's revelation in v17. Both grace and wrath are being revealed simultaneously in the present age through the gospel. The wrath of God is not a future reality only but an active present disclosure, observable in the consequences of human rebellion (vv19-32).

κατεχόντων (katechontōn) — present active participle of katechō, "to hold down, suppress." Not passive ignorance but active suppression: the truth is grasped and then held under. The moral failure described is deliberate, making the charge of suppression rather than mere ignorance fundamental to Paul's indictment.
1.19: φανερόν (phaneron) — "manifest, visible, clear" — the divine disclosure through creation is not obscure or requiring special illumination; it is simply there to be seen. ἐν αὐτοῖς (en autois) — "in them/among them" — the manifestation is not only external (in creation) but internal (written in the conscience, cf. 2:15); the duality of the knowledge makes the suppression all the more culpable.
1.20: ἀόρατα (aorata) — "invisible things," the neuter plural. The invisible attributes are made visible through the visible creation — the paradox is intentional. ἀΐδιος δύναμις (aidios dynamis) — "eternal power" — aidios (eternal) is Paul's only use of this word; it is emphatic for the quality that distinguishes divine power from all created power. θειότης (theiotēs) — "divine nature/divinity" — a different word from theotēs (Godhead, used in Col 2:9); theiotēs refers to the quality of being divine rather than the full personal reality of the Godhead. ἀναπολογήτους (anapologētous) — "without defense, without excuse" — a forensic term; the courtroom image is already present in v18's wrath-language.
1.21:
ἐματαιώθησαν (emataiōthēsan) — aorist passive, "became futile/vain" — the same root as LXX mataiotes (vanity/futility) in Ecclesiastes; Paul describes the darkened mind as the consequence of refusing to honor God. The passive may be a divine passive: they were made futile — not merely that they chose futility but that God ratified their choice.

ἡ ἀσύνετος αὐτῶν καρδία (hē asynchetos kardia) — "their foolish/without-understanding heart." In Jewish anthropology, the kardia is the center of the whole person: will, intellect, and emotion together. The darkening of the heart is the darkening of the entire center of the self, not merely an intellectual error.
1.22: φάσκοντες εἶναι σοφοί (phasontes einai sophoi) — "claiming to be wise" — the present participle describes an ongoing self-designation. The claim to philosophical wisdom (sophia) was the marker of Greco-Roman intellectual culture; Paul's indictment targets the highest cultural achievement of the pagan world, not its lowest.
1.23: ἤλλαξαν τὴν δόξαν (ēllaxan tēn doxan) — "they exchanged the glory" — the aorist active: a definitive act of substitution. The glory that belongs to the incorruptible God is traded for images of corruptible creatures. The exchange-structure (ēllaxan in v23, metēllaxan in vv25-26) frames the moral analysis of the fall.
1.24: παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός (paredoken autous ho theos) — "God gave them over" — the first of three judicial hand-overs (vv24, 26, 28). The aorist active of paradidōmi ("hand over") is not divine passivity (looking away) but active judicial decision: God delivers the rebels into the consequences of their own choice. The same verb describes Christ being handed over to death (4:25; 8:32) — the divine act that operates in both judgment and redemption.
1.25: μετήλλαξαν τὴν ἀλήθειαν τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν τῷ ψεύδει (metēllaxan tēn alētheian tou theou en tō pseudei) — "they exchanged the truth of God for a lie." The exchange completes the pattern of v23: glory exchanged → now truth exchanged. ἐν τῷ ψεύδει — "in/for the lie" — the article tō is notable: not "a lie" but "the lie," suggesting a particular falsehood — the lie of idolatry that claims the creature deserves the Creator's honor.
1.26:
παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός — second occurrence. The repetition of the judicial hand-over formula is structural: Paul traces the escalating consequence of the fall through three stages, each introduced by this phrase. The second stage concerns sexual disorder as both consequence and expression of the inversion of creation order.

φυσικὴν χρῆσιν (physikēn chrēsin) — "natural use" — physis (nature) in Paul typically means the created order as established by God, not individual inclination. The argument is from creation-design rather than from cultural convention.
1.27: ἐξεκαύθησαν ἐν τῇ ὀρέξει αὐτῶν (exekauthēsan en tē orexei autōn) — "burned in their desire/craving" — the aorist passive of ekkaiō (to set ablaze) conveys consuming intensity. τὴν ἀντιμισθίαν ἣν ἔδει τῆς πλάνης αὐτῶν — "the due penalty of their error" — antimisthia (due return, counter-payment) frames the consequence as a moral-cosmic exchange; what they receive is proportional to and appropriate to their departure.
1.28: παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός — third occurrence, completing the structure. εἰς ἀδόκιμον νοῦν (eis adokimon noun) — "into a disqualified/reprobate mind" — adokimos (failing the test, disqualified) is a metallurgical term for metal that does not pass the assay test. The mind that refuses to acknowledge God becomes a mind that cannot reliably distinguish moral categories.
1.29: The vice list in vv29-31 uses four different Greek constructions: (1) πεπληρωμένους (peplērōmenous) — "filled with," perfect passive participle, indicating a settled state; (2) simple nouns in the dative (vv29-31); (3) the -ους suffix adjectives indicating character-type (v29: ponēria, pleonexia, kakia); and (4) the ψιθυριστάς / καταλάλους agent-nouns (v29-30 — "whisperers, slanderers"). The formal variety signals that Paul is not listing mechanically but composing rhetorically.
1.30: θεοστυγεῖς (theostygeis) — "haters of God" or "hated by God." The compound is rare; its active versus passive meaning is debated. The context (a list of human vices) favors "God-haters" — those who actively despise the God they have suppressed (v18). ὑπερηφάνους (hyperēphanous) — "arrogant, haughty" — literally "one who appears above others" (hyper-phainō); the social posturing of pride that appropriates divine prerogative.
1.31: ἀσυνέτους (asynchētous) — "without understanding" — same root as the darkened heart of v21; the disqualified mind of v28 now expresses itself in specific moral categories. ἀσυνθέτους (asynchetous) — "covenant-breakers, untrustworthy" — literally "without-agreement," those who do not keep their pledged word; social trust collapses when the created order's truth is suppressed.
1.32: τὸ δικαίωμα τοῦ θεοῦ (to dikaiōma tou theou) — "the righteous decree/ordinance of God" — not merely a moral intuition but a recognized standard; the pagans know the divine verdict that such practices are death-worthy. οὐ μόνον αὐτὰ ποιοῦσιν — "they not only practice them" — the aggravating factor is συνευδοκοῦσιν (syneudokousin) — "they also approve of/applaud" those who do them. The moral dysfunction has become cultural: the vice is celebrated, not merely committed.

2.1: ὦ ἄνθρωπε πᾶς ὁ κρίνων (ō anthrōpe pas ho krinōn) — "O man, every one who judges" — the vocative ō is rhetorical, addressing a generic figure (the moralistic judge). Paul does not yet specify Jew or Gentile, though the argument progressively reveals he has the morally self-confident Jew particularly in mind by vv17-20. ἀναπολόγητος — "without defense" — the same word as 1:20; the judge who condemns others on the basis of the knowledge that he himself suppresses is equally without excuse.
2.2: κρίμα τοῦ θεοῦ (krima tou theou) — "judgment of God" — a judicial noun (krima = the act of judging, or the verdict rendered). κατὰ ἀλήθειαν (kata alētheian) — "according to truth" — God's judgment is measured against truth (the standard he himself is), not against the self-defined moral systems of the judge; the contrast with human hypocrisy is built into the standard.
2.3: λογίζῃ (logizē) — "do you reckon/calculate?" — the same forensic term Paul uses in 3:28 and 4:3-8 for imputation. Here it is ironic: the judge is calculating/reasoning incorrectly about divine judgment, imagining that moral posturing exempts from the verdict that comes on those who do the same things.
2.4:
χρηστότητος (chrēstotētos), ἀνοχῆς (anochēs), μακροθυμίας (makrothymias) — three distinct Greek terms for divine patience: (1) chrēstotēs = kindness, moral goodness in disposition; (2) anochē = forbearance, the restraint of wrath that could be exercised; (3) makrothymia = long-suffering, enduring capacity not to retaliate. Their accumulation signals that Paul is not using synonyms loosely but cataloguing the specific qualities of divine patience.

εἰς μετάνοιαν (eis metanoian) — "toward repentance" — the purpose of divine kindness is the behavioral-and-cognitive turning that metanoia denotes (meta = after, noia = mind; the after-mind, the changed perspective that produces changed conduct).
2.5: σκληρότητα καὶ ἀμετανόητον καρδίαν (sklērotēta kai ametanoēton kardian) — "hardness and unrepentant heart" — sklērotēs (hardness) echoes the OT hardening of Pharaoh's heart; ametanoētos (unrepentant) is Paul's only use of this adjective. The irony is that the one who calls others to repentance has an unrepentant heart. ὀργῆς (orgēs) — "wrath" — the day of wrath is a treasury being accumulated by every act of unrepentant hardness: θησαυρίζεις (thēsaurizeis) = "you are treasuring up" (present active), an active storing of future judgment.
2.6: ἀποδώσει ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ (apodōsei hekastō kata ta erga autou) — "he will render to each according to his works" — the future active of apodidōmi (to give back what is owed). The standard is individual works rather than group membership. Paul is not setting up a works-salvation principle that will later be qualified by grace; he is first establishing that God's judgment is impartial and individual, which is essential before revealing that none can meet the standard (3:20).
2.7: ζητοῦσιν δόξαν καὶ τιμὴν καὶ ἀφθαρσίαν (zētousin doxan kai timēn kai aphtharsian) — "seeking glory and honor and immortality" — the three nouns move from present experience to future reality. Aphtharsian (incorruptibility) is the eschatological dimension: the transformed body not subject to the decay of the present creation. Paul's description of eternal life (v7) is emphatically concrete and embodied.
2.8: ἐξ ἐριθείας (ex eritheias) — "from selfish ambition" — eritheia originally meant "work for hire" and came to denote the motivation of mercenary self-seeking; it appears in the vice lists of Gal 5:20 and Phil 2:3. Those characterized by it are contrasted with those who seek glory (v7) — the moral contrast is between self-seeking and God-seeking as the organizing principle of one's life.
2.9: Ἰουδαίῳ τε πρῶτον καὶ Ἕλληνι — the Jew-first priority in v16 (grace) is now applied to tribulation: covenant privilege brings covenant accountability. The prōton (first) is symmetrical — the primacy is in both directions, which is why Paul says it in both 1:16 and 2:9-10.
2.10: δόξα δὲ καὶ τιμὴ καὶ εἰρήνη (doxa de kai timē kai eirēnē) — the three goods of v7 (glory, honor) are repeated with eirēnē (peace) substituted for aphtharsian (immortality); peace is the present-tense experience of what immortality will complete. The parallel structuring of vv9-10 by the repeated Ἰουδαίῳ τε πρῶτον καὶ Ἕλληνι reinforces the impartiality principle.
2.11: προσωπολημψία (prosōpolēmpsia) — "face-taking" — a Hebrew idiom (nasa panim = lift the face toward someone in favor) translated into Greek. The divine impartiality Paul asserts is a direct echo of Deut 10:17 ("the LORD your God does not show favoritism") applied now to Jewish-Gentile distinctions in divine judgment.
2.12: ἀνόμως ἥμαρτον, ἀνόμως καὶ ἀπολοῦνται (anomōs hēmarton, anomōs kai apolountai) — "sinned without law... will perish without law." The anōmos / en nomō contrast structures the principle of judgment by the available standard. Gentiles are not judged against a law they did not have; they are judged by what they did have (the natural law of vv14-15).
2.13: ποιηταὶ νόμου δικαιωθήσονται (poiētai nomou dikaiōthēsontai) — "doers of the law will be justified." The future passive of dikaioō is forensic: declared righteous at the eschatological judgment. Paul is stating the principle of Lev 18:5 seriously before showing in 3:9-20 that no one is actually such a doer; the principle stands but the actuality refutes all claimants.
2.14: φύσει (physei) — "by nature" — a dative of manner, not dative of origin (not "by birth" but "by the nature of things"). Gentiles who do instinctively what the law requires demonstrate that natural moral knowledge exists independently of Sinai's revelation. ἑαυτοῖς εἰσιν νόμος (heautois eisin nomos) — "they are a law to themselves" — not moral relativism but the affirmation that the law's normative claim is written into human nature.
2.15: συνειδήσεως (syneidēseōs) — "conscience" — from syn-oida (to know together with); the conscience is the faculty of co-knowing, the internal witness that evaluates one's own actions by a shared moral standard. τῶν λογισμῶν κατηγορούντων ἢ καὶ ἀπολογουμένων — "the thoughts accusing or defending" — the forensic vocabulary (accuse/defend) is explicit: the conscience conducts an interior trial.
2.16: ἐν ᾗ ἡμέρᾳ (en hē hēmera) — "on the day when" — the relative clause connects back to v13's principle; the justification of v13 will occur at the eschatological judgment. διὰ Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ — "through Christ Jesus" — the divine judge of the secrets of humanity operates through the mediatorial agency of Christ, unexpected in the context of universal moral judgment but consistent with Paul's Christological totality.
2.17: Ἰουδαῖος ἐπονομάζῃ (Ioudaios eponomazē) — "you call yourself a Jew" — the present middle indicative; the self-designation is a claim being made repeatedly. From vv17-20 Paul lists five privileges the Jew claims: (1) the name/identity, (2) reliance on Torah, (3) boasting in God, (4) knowing God's will, (5) discerning what matters. The list sets up the irony of vv21-24.
2.18: δοκιμάζεις τὰ διαφέροντα (dokimazeis ta diapheronta) — "you approve/discern what matters" — dokimazō is the testing/assaying term (see 1:28); ta diapheronta can mean "the things that differ" (distinguishing between options) or "the things that are superior" (preferring what matters). The ambiguity is likely intentional: the instruction of the law provides both distinction and prioritization.
2.19: ὁδηγὸν εἶναι τυφλῶν (hodēgon einai typhlōn) — "a guide to the blind" — hodēgos = road-guide (hodos + agō). The claim echoes Isa 42:6-7 (the Servant as a light to the blind and a covenant to the nations); Paul uses the self-designation of the Servant to describe the Jew's self-understanding, setting up the irony that the guide needs guiding.
2.20: μορφωσιν τῆς γνώσεως καὶ τῆς ἀληθείας (morphōsin tēs gnōseōs kai tēs alētheias) — "the form of knowledge and truth" — morphōsis denotes the external shape or appearance of something; Paul's choice of this word is already critical: the law gives the Jewish teacher an external form (morphōsis) of knowledge without necessarily the inner reality (morphē would be the real form).
2.21: κηρύσσων μὴ κλέπτειν κλέπτεις (kēryssōn mē kleptein klepteis) — "you who preach not to steal, do you steal?" — the present active participle (preaching) paired with the present indicative (stealing) creates the indictment of simultaneous proclamation and violation. The preacher-thief is not hypothetical but representative of a recognized pattern in Paul's indictment.
2.22: βδελυσσόμενος τὰ εἴδωλα ἱεροσυλεῖς (bdelyssomenos ta eidōla hierosyleis) — "you who abhor idols, do you rob temples?" — hierosyleis (to rob a temple) could refer to literal sacrilege (looting pagan shrines while professing to detest idolatry) or to the broader spiritual idolatry of covetousness Paul will address elsewhere. Either way, the verbal irony is sharp.
2.23: νόμου παράβασις (nomou parabasis) — "transgression of the law" — parabasis (stepping alongside, stepping past) is more deliberate than mere failure (hamartia); it implies knowing the boundary and crossing it intentionally. This is the specific accusation: not ignorance of the law but active violation of the law one claims to teach.
2.24: τὸ γὰρ ὄνομα τοῦ θεοῦ δι᾽ ὑμᾶς βλασφημεῖται — "for the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you" — a quotation from LXX Isa 52:5 (cf. Ezek 36:20-22). The present passive blasphēmeitai indicates an ongoing reality, not a historical incident; the Jewish community's covenant violations have become a sustained cause of Gentile contempt for the God who claims them.
2.25: περιτομὴ μὲν γὰρ ὠφελεῖ (peritomē men gar ōphelei) — "circumcision benefits" — Paul affirms the real value of circumcision before qualifying it; the men gar construction acknowledges genuine advantage. ἐὰν νόμον πράσσῃς — "if you practice the law" — the conditional shifts the benefit from the rite itself to the covenantal obedience it signs; the sign without the signified reality is null.
2.26: ἡ ἀκροβυστία αὐτοῦ εἰς περιτομὴν λογισθήσεται (hē akrobystia autou eis peritomēn logisthēsetai) — "his uncircumcision will be reckoned as circumcision" — logizō again: the forensic reckoning that counts uncircumcision as circumcision when covenant faithfulness is present. Paul applies the same reckoning-logic he will use for Abraham's faith in ch4 to the Gentile who keeps the law's requirements.
2.27: κρινεῖ (krinei) — future active, "will judge" — the eschatological scenario: the obedient Gentile standing as a judge over the disobedient, law-possessing Jew. The reversal echoes the OT tradition of Nineveh's repentance condemning Israel (Matt 12:41); external markers of covenant membership without covenant faithfulness cannot survive the judgment.
2.28: ἐν τῷ κρυπτῷ Ἰουδαῖος (en tō kryptō Ioudaios) — "in the hidden Jew" — kryptos (hidden, inward) contrasts with the external/visible (phaneros, v28). Paul's paradox: the real Jew is the one who is a Jew in the hidden, interior reality — a formulation that both affirms Jewish particularity and universalizes it by grounding Jewishness in the inward reality that the covenant was always aimed at (Deut 30:6 — circumcision of the heart).
2.29: περιτομὴ καρδίας ἐν πνεύματι οὐ γράμματι (peritomē kardias en pneumati ou grammati) — "circumcision of heart, in Spirit not letter" — the pneuma/gramma (spirit/letter) antithesis Paul will develop in 7:6 and 2 Cor 3:6. The circumcision the OT promises (Deut 30:6; Jer 4:4; Ezek 36:26-27) is the interior reality of which the external rite is a sign. ἔπαινος οὐκ ἐξ ἀνθρώπων ἀλλ᾽ ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ — praise from God rather than humans is the ultimate validation — a direct inversion of the honor-shame framework that makes human praise the measure of worth.

3.1: τί οὖν τὸ περισσὸν τοῦ Ἰουδαίου (ti oun to perisson tou Ioudaiou) — "what then is the advantage of the Jew?" — Paul shifts to a diatribal interlocutor. Perisson means "excess, advantage, surplus" — the same root as perisseuo (to abound). The question acknowledges that the argument of ch2 might seem to eliminate Jewish advantage entirely; Paul will affirm real advantage before qualifying it.
3.2: ἐπιστεύθησαν τὰ λόγια τοῦ θεοῦ (episteuthēsan ta logia tou theou) — "they were entrusted with the oracles of God" — the aorist passive of pisteuō (to believe/entrust); the same word-family as faith but in the trust-deposit sense. Logia (oracles) is a term for divine utterances, particularly prophetic speech. The advantage is not ethnicity but covenant revelation: having been entrusted with God's own words.
3.3: ἡ ἀπιστία αὐτῶν τὴν πίστιν τοῦ θεοῦ καταργήσει (hē apistia autōn tēn pistin tou theou katargēsei) — "will their unbelief nullify God's faithfulness?" — apistia (unbelief/unfaithfulness) contrasted with pistis tou theou (faithfulness of God). The genitive tou theou here is clearly subjective: God's own faithfulness/trustworthiness. Katargeō — "to render inoperative, nullify" — a strong verb Paul uses for the abolition of the law and the nullification of Christ (Gal 5:4).
3.4: γινέσθω δὲ ὁ θεὸς ἀληθής (ginesthō de ho theos alēthēs) — "let God be true" — the imperative ginesthō (become/let-be) does not mean God is not yet true but is the rhetorical assertion of the absolute: even if (hypothetically) all humans were liars, God's truth stands unaffected. ὅπως ἂν δικαιωθῇς ἐν τοῖς λόγοις σου — quoting LXX Ps 51:4: "that you may be justified in your words" — the psalmist's confession that God is right when he judges applies to the present argument.
3.5: τί ἐροῦμεν; μὴ ἄδικος ὁ θεός (ti eroumen; mē adikos ho theos) — "what shall we say? Is God unjust?" — the interlocutor's reductio: if my injustice displays God's righteousness, how can God judge me? Paul identifies the reasoning immediately as distinctively human (κατὰ ἄνθρωπον λέγω — "I speak as a human being"). The objection is logically coherent but morally absurd.
3.6: ἐπεὶ πῶς κρινεῖ ὁ θεὸς τὸν κόσμον — "since how will God judge the world?" — the rhetorical appeal to universal divine judgment as self-evident: if God cannot judge fairly, the entire structure of biblical theodicy collapses. The world's judgment as God's prerogative is so foundational that the objection refutes itself.
3.7: ἐν τῷ ἐμῷ ψεύσματι (en tō emō pseudismati) — "through my lie" — Paul continues the diatribal exchange, now pushing the logic to its most absurd conclusion: if my falsehood increases God's truth-display, I should be more praised, not condemned. The ema (my own) is emphatic: this is the interlocutor's own reasoning about his own falsehood.
3.8: ποιήσωμεν τὰ κακὰ ἵνα ἔλθῃ τὰ ἀγαθά — "let us do evil that good may come" — this is not Paul's teaching but the slander leveled against Paul's gospel of grace; some were evidently claiming that his teaching logically implied moral libertinism. ὧν τὸ κρίμα ἔνδικόν ἐστιν — "whose condemnation is just" — endikos (in-right, just) pronounces the condemnation fitting by the law's own standard.
3.9:
οὐ πάντως (ou pantōs) — "not at all / not entirely." The phrase is debated: does Paul say Jews have "no advantage at all" (contradicting v2) or "no advantage in every respect"? Given v2's "much in every way," Paul likely means "no decisive advantage for the purpose of avoiding condemnation" — not a flat denial of all Jewish advantage.

προῃτιασάμεθα (proētiasametha) — "we have already charged" — the perfect tense indicates the charge is established from prior argument; Paul now summarizes. The verb aitiaō is forensic: to bring a formal charge.
3.10: The catena of quotations in vv10-18 is a classical Jewish rhetorical technique of stringing OT texts together to make a cumulative point (cf. 4QFlor at Qumran). The individual quotations are from Ps 14/53; Ps 5:9; Ps 140:3; Ps 10:7; Isa 59:7-8; Ps 36:1 — spanning Psalms and Prophets to demonstrate that the indictment is not Pauline invention but Scripture's own verdict on universal human unrighteousness.
3.11: οὐκ ἔστιν ὁ συνίων, οὐκ ἔστιν ὁ ἐκζητῶν τὸν θεόν — "there is none who understands, none who seeks God" — syniōn (understanding, from synesis) and ekzētōn (seeking out, intensive compound of zēteō). The double negation and the present participles make these absolute characterizations: understanding and God-seeking are entirely absent from human initiative in the natural state. Paul quotes Ps 14:2 LXX verbatim.
3.12: πάντες ἐξέκλιναν (pantes exeklinan) — "all have turned aside" — the aorist active: a completed definitive turning. Exklinō (turn out from, deviate) is a navigational metaphor: everyone has gone off course. ἅμα (hama) — "together/at once" — universality is both numerical (all) and simultaneous (together), leaving no exception.
3.13: τάφος ἀνεῳγμένος (taphos aneōgmenos) — "an open grave" — perfect passive participle: permanently open. The throat as an open grave is the image of consuming speech that destroys what it consumes; Ps 5:9 LXX provides this image. ἐδολιοῦσαν ταῖς γλώσσαις αὐτῶν (edoliousan tais glōssais autōn) — "with their tongues they were deceitful" — imperfect tense: ongoing habitual deceit. The speech-anatomy chain (throat, tongue, lips, mouth) moves from the largest organ to the smallest.
3.14: οὗ τὸ στόμα ἀρᾶς καὶ πικρίας γέμει (hou to stoma aras kai pikrias gemei) — "whose mouth is full of cursing and bitterness" — the present active gemei (is full to the brim) indicates a saturated, overflowing condition; not occasional outburst but characteristic speech. From Ps 10:7 LXX.
3.15: ὀξεῖς οἱ πόδες αὐτῶν ἐκχέαι αἷμα (oxeis hoi podes autōn ekcheai haima) — "their feet are swift to shed blood" — oxys (sharp, swift) describes a readiness that requires no deliberation; violence is the instinctive trajectory of the unrenewed will. From Isa 59:7-8 LXX, where the same indictment falls on Israel specifically.
3.16: σύντριμμα καὶ ταλαιπωρία (syntrimma kai talaipōria) — "destruction and misery" — syntrimma (a crushing together, from trib = rubbing) is what the swift feet leave behind; the violence that the tongue and feet produce creates a trail of crushed and suffering people. The social consequences of universal unrighteousness are not abstract but embodied in human suffering.
3.17: ὁδὸν εἰρήνης οὐκ ἔγνωσαν (hodon eirēnēs ouk egnōsan) — "the way of peace they have not known" — the aorist egnōsan (they knew) marks this as a definitively absent knowledge; shalom as the experience of right relationships (with God, self, neighbor, creation) is unknown to the unrighteous. The road imagery reinforces the navigational language of v12 (all turned aside).
3.18: οὐκ ἔστιν φόβος θεοῦ ἀπέναντι τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν αὐτῶν (ouk estin phobos theou apenanti tōn ophthalmōn autōn) — "there is no fear of God before their eyes" — phobos theou (fear of God) is the OT term for the foundational orientation of wisdom and righteousness (Ps 111:10; Prov 1:7; 9:10). Its absence before their eyes is the summary diagnosis: the entire inner life is organized without reference to the holy God. Quoting Ps 36:1 LXX.
3.19: ἵνα πᾶν στόμα φραγῇ (hina pan stoma phragē) — "that every mouth may be stopped" — aorist passive subjunctive of phrassō (to fence off, block); the closing of every mouth before God is the forensic consequence of the catena: the OT's own verdict leaves no room for self-defense. ὑπόδικος γένηται πᾶς ὁ κόσμος τῷ θεῷ — "that all the world may become accountable to God" — hypodikos = "under sentence, answerable to" (hypo = under; dikē = justice/judgment).
3.20:
ἐξ ἔργων νόμου οὐ δικαιωθήσεται πᾶσα σάρξ (ex ergōn nomou ou dikaiōthēsetai pasa sarx) — "from works of law no flesh will be justified" — the future passive declarative: the eschatological judgment will produce no acquittals on the basis of law-performance. πᾶσα σάρξ (pasa sarx) — "all flesh" — LXX idiom for every human being, emphasizing the frailty and mortality of the human creature before the holy God.

διὰ γὰρ νόμου ἐπίγνωσις ἁμαρτίας (dia gar nomou epignōsis hamartias) — "for through the law comes knowledge of sin" — not gnōsis (knowledge) but epignōsis (full/accurate knowledge); the law does not merely inform about sin but produces a precise recognition of its character and extent. This is the law's true diagnostic function — not justification but conviction.
3.21:
νυνὶ δέ (nyni de) — "but now" — the most emphatic temporal hinge in the letter; the de (but/and) marks the adversative contrast with the law's inability (v20), and nyni (now) marks the new epoch. The disclosure of God's righteousness is both temporal (happening now in the present era) and logical (here is the alternative to works-of-law).

χωρὶς νόμου (chōris nomou) — "apart from law" — not "against law" or "replacing law" but "independent of law as a mechanism of acquittal." The preposition chōris (apart from, without) is precise: the law is not the instrument through which this righteousness operates. μαρτυρουμένη (marturoumenē) — "being witnessed to" — present passive participle; the law and prophets are not being bypassed but are themselves bearing witness to what is now disclosed.
3.22: πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (pisteōs Iēsou Christou) — the central syntactical debate: is this "faith in Jesus Christ" (objective genitive, the traditional reading) or "faithfulness of Jesus Christ" (subjective genitive, understanding Christ himself as the faithful one)? Grammatically both are possible; contextually Paul's consistent emphasis on the human act of believing (v22b: "to all who believe") favors the objective reading, but the subjective reading captures something theologically true (Christ's own faithfulness in obedience to death) that Paul develops in 5:12-21.
3.23: πάντες γὰρ ἥμαρτον (pantes gar hēmarton) — "for all sinned" — aorist indicative: a single historical act encompassing all humanity. The aorist may point to the Adam-event (all sinned in Adam, 5:12) rather than merely to individual moral failures. ὑστεροῦνται τῆς δόξης τοῦ θεοῦ (hysterounta tēs doxēs tou theou) — "fall short of the glory of God" — present middle/passive, continuous state. The glory of God lost in Adam's fall is the OT background (kebod yhwh); Paul sees the present condition of humanity as the ongoing consequence of that original loss.
3.24:
δωρεὰν (dōrean) — "freely, as a gift, gratis." The adverb emphasizes that the justification costs the recipient nothing — it is entirely given without merit or payment on the receiver's part. Notably the same word appears in John 15:25 ("they hated me without reason") and John 1:16 ("grace upon grace"); dōrean means "at no cost" to the one receiving.

ἀπολυτρώσεως (apolytrōseōs) — "redemption" — the lytron root means ransom price paid to secure release (of a slave or prisoner of war). The apo- prefix emphasizes the resultant freedom: the redemption that liberates. The price is specified in v25 (Christ's blood); the mechanism (the propitiation) and the means (faith) together constitute the ground of the free justification.
3.25:
ἱλαστήριον (hilastērion) — the most debated single term in Romans. Three primary options: (1) the mercy seat (kapporeth) of Exod 25:17-22 and Lev 16, where the LXX uses this exact word — making Christ the place where God and humanity meet in the blood-applied atonement; (2) a propitiatory sacrifice (the sin-covering/guilt-removing offering); (3) a means of expiation (removing the defilement of sin without the propitiation element). The mercy seat background has the advantage of being the LXX term and providing a spatial-priestly image: Christ is both the priest and the meeting-place of divine presence and human need.

ἐν τῇ ἀνοχῇ τοῦ θεοῦ (en tē anochē tou theou) — "in the forbearance of God" — the same word as 2:4; divine patience during the period of prior sins was not moral indifference but the postponement of the full accounting until the cross. The OT sacrificial system operated on "credit" secured by the coming atonement.
3.26: εἰς τὸ εἶναι αὐτὸν δίκαιον καὶ δικαιοῦντα τὸν ἐκ πίστεως Ἰησοῦ (eis to einai auton dikaion kai dikaiounta ton ek pisteōs Iēsou) — "so that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus." The pair dikaion kai dikaiounta resolves the divine dilemma: the cross satisfies the demand of God's holiness (he remains just) while simultaneously enabling the acquittal of the ungodly (he is the justifier). Without the cross, God could be just or merciful but not both simultaneously; the atonement is the resolution of this tension in Paul's theology.
3.27: διὰ ποίου νόμου; (dia poiou nomou;) — "through what kind of law?" — the unexpected use of nomos for the "law of faith" is a wordplay: the law of works and the law of faith are both nomoi (principles) but of opposite character. Faith as a nomos excludes boasting by its very nature — it credits what is given, not what is earned. ἐξεκλείσθη (exekleisthē) — aorist passive: boasting has been definitively shut out, excluded (from the same root as the shutting of every mouth in v19).
3.28: λογιζόμεθα γὰρ δικαιοῦσθαι πίστει ἄνθρωπον (logizometha gar dikaiousthai pistei anthrōpon) — "for we reckon that a person is justified by faith." Logizometha (present middle: we are reckoning, we count it as established) is the same forensic-accounting verb that governs the whole argument of ch4. χωρὶς ἔργων νόμου — "apart from works of law" — Paul will add "alone" by implication; Luther's famous sola fide translation ("by faith alone") is a legitimate interpretive rendering of the choris ergōn nomou exclusion.
3.29: ἢ Ἰουδαίων ὁ θεὸς μόνον; (ē Ioudaiōn ho theos monon;) — "or is God the God of Jews only?" — the rhetorical question surfaces the implication of the faith-principle: a God who justifies by faith (rather than by ethnic-covenant membership) is by definition a God of all peoples who believe. The monotheism of v30 ("since God is one") is the theological ground of the universality of faith-justification.
3.30: εἴπερ εἷς ὁ θεός (eiper heis ho theos) — "since indeed God is one" — the Shema (Deut 6:4 — yhwh echad) is the ground of Paul's argument for universal salvation by faith. One God means one way of right-standing before that God — faith — applicable to Jew and Gentile alike. The monotheism of Israel's confession becomes the basis for the universality of the gospel.
3.31: νόμον οὖν καταργοῦμεν διὰ τῆς πίστεως; (nomon oun katargoumen dia tēs pisteōs;) — "do we therefore nullify the law through faith?" — katargeō (render inoperative) — the anticipated objection after ch3's argument. Paul's denial is emphatic: μὴ γένοιτο· ἀλλὰ νόμον ἱστάνομεν — "by no means! On the contrary, we establish the law." Histanomen (we establish/uphold) — faith does not abolish the law but fulfills the purpose the law was always pointing toward, and confirms the law's own testimony (3:21 — the law and prophets witnessed to this righteousness).

11.1: μὴ ἀπώσατο ὁ θεὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ — The aorist middle apōsato (push away, reject) anticipates the expected conclusion from ch. 10's hard language. Paul's immediate counter-evidence is autobiographical: καὶ γὰρ ἐγὼ Ἰσραηλίτης εἰμί — "For I too am an Israelite." The kai is emphatic: I myself am exhibit A of an un-rejected Israelite. The three-part pedigree — seed of Abraham, tribe of Benjamin — mirrors the pattern of Phil 3:5, where the same credentials appear as grounds for confidence "in the flesh."
11.2: οὐκ ἀπώσατο ὁ θεὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ ὃν προέγνω — The relative clause hon proegnō (whom he foreknew) is the crux: election precedes any historical contingency, so rejection is structurally impossible. The verb proginōskō (G4267) in Paul never means mere foreknowledge in the informational sense; it means prior choice establishing relationship. Psalm 94:14 ("the Lord will not forsake his people") provides the ground; the Elijah episode provides the illustration.
11.3: Κύριε, τοὺς προφήτας σου ἀπέκτειναν — The quotation follows 1 Kgs 19:10/14 (LXX). Elijah's complaint is the voice of prophetic despair: altars torn down, prophets killed, only I left (κἀγὼ ὑπελείφθην μόνος). Paul uses this as a type of the present crisis — the faithful remnant feels isolated, yet the divine answer will reveal otherwise.
11.4: κατέλιπον ἐμαυτῷ ἑπτακισχιλίους ἄνδρας — The divine response uses the perfect katelipon (I have kept/reserved for myself): the remnant is not self-selected but divinely preserved. The dative emautō — for myself — establishes the remnant as God's possession, not simply survivors. The phrase "who have not bowed the knee to Baal" is a concrete behavioral test; the negative formulation (a knee not bent) serves as the criterion.
11.5: λεῖμμα κατ᾽ ἐκλογὴν χάριτος γέγονεν — Leimma (G3005, remnant) picks up the Elijah language. Kat᾽ eklogēn charitos — according to election of grace: the genitive charitos qualifies the election. The perfect gegonen (has come to be and stands) marks the present reality of the remnant as established fact, not aspiration.
11.6: εἰ δὲ χάριτι, οὐκέτι ἐξ ἔργων — A logical disjunction Paul has already established in ch. 4 with Abraham: grace and works are mutually exclusive bases for the same standing. Epei hē charis ouketi ginetai charis — otherwise grace is no longer grace. The epei (since/otherwise) clause states the consequence of mixing the two: the category dissolves. Paul's argument is analytic, not moral: by definition, grace-based election cannot be simultaneously works-based.
11.7: τοῦτο οὗ ἐπιζητεῖ Ἰσραήλ, τοῦτο οὐκ ἐπέτυχεν — Epizēteō (G1934, seek earnestly) implies persistent pursuit. Epitykhein (to hit upon, attain, G2013) is the athletic-competition word for reaching the goal. Israel pursued but did not obtain; the elect obtained. οἱ δὲ λοιποὶ ἐπωρώθησαν — the rest were hardened: the divine passive of pōroō (G4456) — to calcify, to form a callous. The cognate noun pōrōsis (v.25) denotes the resulting condition.
11.8: ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ὁ θεὸς πνεῦμα κατανύξεως — The citation blends Deut 29:4 and Isa 29:10 (LXX). Katanyksis (G2659) is a hapax in the NT: stupor, numbness, a word for the deep insensibility induced by divine judgment. In Isa 29:10 the parallel is "the spirit of deep sleep." Paul attributes the stupor to God's giving (edōken, aorist), not to Israel's self-infliction — consistent with the divine-passive hardening of v.7.
11.9: Δαυὶδ λέγει· γενηθήτω ἡ τράπεζα αὐτῶν εἰς παγίδα — Ps 69:22–23 (LXX 68:22–23). The table (trapeza) that should provide sustenance becomes a snare (pagis) and a trap (thēra). Paul's use of David is deliberate: the Psalm is a suffering-righteous Psalm widely applied to Jesus in early Christianity (Ps 69:9 at John 2:17; 69:21 at Matt 27:34/John 19:29). The application to Israel's hardening draws from the same Psalm that elsewhere illuminates Christ's own rejection.
11.10: σκοτισθήτωσαν οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτῶν τοῦ μὴ βλέπειν — The continuation of Ps 69:23: darkened eyes and permanently bent backs. The articular infinitive tou mē blepein expresses purpose or result — eyes darkened so as not to see. Dia pantos (continually, always) indicates the sustained character of the judgment, not a temporary condition. The image of the bent back under load closes the citation with a picture of servitude.
11.11: μὴ ἔπταισαν ἵνα πέσωσιν — The distinction is between ptaiō (G4417, to stumble) and piptō (G4098, to fall definitively). Did they stumble so as to fall irrecoverably? Mē genoito — by no means. The word paraptōmati (transgression, G3900) appears: their stumbling was a paraptoōma that occasioned Gentile inclusion. Parazēlōsai (G3863, to provoke to jealousy) picks up the motif from Deut 32:21 (cited at 10:19): Israel's jealousy of Gentile blessing is part of the restorative plan.
11.12: εἰ δὲ τὸ παράπτωμα αὐτῶν πλοῦτος κόσμου — Paul constructs a qal wachomer (light-to-heavy) argument: if their failure (παράπτωμα) produces world-wealth, how much more will their plērōma produce? The word plērōma (G4138, fullness) here means their completion — their full inclusion. The contrast is παράπτωμα/ἥττημα (failure/defeat) against plērōma. The conclusion of the argument will come at v.26.
11.13: ὑμῖν λέγω τοῖς ἔθνεσιν — Paul pivots to direct address to Gentile readers. He asserts his apostolic identity: ἐφ᾽ ὅσον μέν εἰμι ἐγὼ ἐθνῶν ἀπόστολος — inasmuch as I am apostle of the Gentiles. The present tense eimi is timeless. δοξάζω τὴν διακονίαν μου — I glorify my ministry: the verb doxazō does not mean boasting; it means to recognize and honor the worth of what has been entrusted.
11.14: εἴ πως παραζηλώσω μου τὴν σάρκα — The optative parazelōsō (might provoke to jealousy) expresses contingent hope, not certainty. Tēn sarka mou — my flesh, i.e., my kinsmen according to biological descent (cf. 9:3). The chain of purpose: Paul's Gentile mission creates Gentile blessing → Gentile blessing provokes Jewish jealousy → jealousy leads some to salvation. Paul presents his Gentile apostolate as indirectly missionary to Israel.
11.15: εἰ γὰρ ἡ ἀποβολὴ αὐτῶν καταλλαγὴ κόσμου — Apobolē (G580, casting away, rejection) is a rare word. Katallagē (G2643, reconciliation) appeared at 5:11; it denotes the restored relational status between God and the world. If their rejection produced reconciliation, what will their acceptance produce? The answer: ζωὴ ἐκ νεκρῶν — life from the dead. Whether this refers to eschatological resurrection or to a spiritual revival of unprecedented scale, Paul leaves the image to press the point: the scale is incomprehensible.
11.16: εἰ δὲ ἡ ἀπαρχὴ ἁγία, καὶ τὸ φύραμα — Aparkhē (G536, firstfruit) and phyrama (G5445, lump of dough) draw on Num 15:20–21 where the dough offering consecrates the whole batch. The argument is one of representative holiness: the part that is holy sanctifies the whole. Paul applies the image in two registers: firstfruit/lump (temporal — the patriarchs sanctify the nation) and root/branches (organic — the root determines the character of what it supports).
11.17: εἰ δέ τινες τῶν κλάδων ἐξεκλάσθησαν — Exeklasthēsan (were broken off, aorist passive) marks a completed act. The Gentile reader is addressed as a ἀγριέλαιος (G65, wild olive, hapax in NT) grafted in ἐν αὐτοῖς (among them, the remaining branches). The shared benefit is τῆς ῥίζης τῆς πιότητος τῆς ἐλαίας — the root of the fatness (richness) of the olive tree. Piotēs (G4096, fatness, richness) is agricultural: the root's nutrient supply feeds all grafted branches equally.
11.18: μὴ κατακαυχῶ τῶν κλάδων — Katakaukhaō (G2620, to boast against, to exult over) is the specific wrong. The corrective is logical: οὐ σὺ τὴν ῥίζαν βαστάζεις ἀλλ᾽ ἡ ῥίζα σέ — you do not carry the root; the root carries you. The Gentile believer's continuance depends on the root (the patriarchal covenant/promise), not vice versa. The present tense bastazeis/se marks an ongoing structural reality.
11.19: ἐρεῖς οὖν — Paul quotes the imagined Gentile interlocutor: "Branches were broken off so that I might be grafted in." The passive exeklasthēsan (were broken off) is used again; the purpose hina egō enkentristhō presumes the Gentile's grafting was the goal of Israel's breaking off. Paul will grant the premise in v.20 but challenge the inference.
11.20: καλῶς· τῇ ἀπιστίᾳ ἐξεκλάσθησαν — Kalōs (well, rightly) concedes the factual claim: yes, broken off by unbelief. σὺ δὲ τῇ πίστει ἕστηκας — the dative of means: you stand by faith. The perfect hestēkas (you have stood and stand) marks a stable condition. The warning follows: μὴ ὑψηλὰ φρόνει ἀλλὰ φοβοῦ — do not think high thoughts but fear. Hypsēla phronein (to think loftily) is the same root as hypselophrosynē condemned in 12:16.
11.21: εἰ γὰρ ὁ θεὸς τῶν κατὰ φύσιν κλάδων οὐκ ἐφείσατο — The aorist epheisato (spared, G5339): God did not spare the natural branches — those that belonged to the tree by origin. The argument is from the greater to the lesser: if not the natural, then certainly not you. The phrase κατὰ φύσιν (according to nature) reappears from 11:17 and anticipates the παρὰ φύσιν (against nature) of v.24 — the surprise of the Gentile grafting.
11.22: ἴδε οὖν χρηστότητα καὶ ἀποτομίαν θεοῦ — Paul juxtaposes chrēstotēs (G5544, kindness/goodness) and apotomia (G663, severity/steepness; from apotemnō, to cut off sharply). The latter is a hapax in the NT; it denotes abruptness, the quality of an abrupt cut. The condition for continued Gentile standing: ἐὰν ἐπιμένῃς τῇ χρηστότητι — if you remain in the kindness. The conditional ean + present subjunctive marks genuine contingency: the standing is real but not unconditional in its continuation.
11.23: ἐκεῖνοι δέ, ἐὰν μὴ ἐπιμένωσιν τῇ ἀπιστίᾳ, ἐγκεντρισθήσονται — The condition for Israel's re-grafting is removal of unbelief (apistia). The future passive enkentristhēsontai (will be grafted in) is the divine act awaiting the condition. The ground: δυνατὸς γάρ ἐστιν ὁ θεὸς πάλιν ἐγκεντρίσαι αὐτούς — God is able again to graft them in. Dynatos (G1415, powerful/able) emphasizes divine capacity against any human calculation that re-grafting is impossible.
11.24: εἰ γὰρ σὺ ἐκ τῆς κατὰ φύσιν ἐξεκόπης ἀγριελαίου — Paul now explicitly names the Gentile situation as παρὰ φύσιν (against nature): the wild olive being grafted into the cultivated tree defies normal horticultural logic (the cultivated is grafted into rootstock, not the other direction). If the unnatural grafting happened, the natural re-grafting is a fortiori — πολλῷ μᾶλλον (how much more) — achievable. The argument closes the olive tree metaphor.
11.25:
οὐ θέλω ὑμᾶς ἀγνοεῖν, ἀδελφοί, τὸ μυστήριον τοῦτο — The disclosure formula (ou thelō hymas agnoein — I do not want you to be ignorant) marks a formal revelation. Mystērion (G3466) in Pauline usage is not a puzzle but something formerly hidden and now divinely disclosed (cf. 16:25–26; Col 1:26–27). The content: partial hardening (πώρωσις ἀπὸ μέρους) has come upon Israel until (ἄχρι οὗ) the fullness of the Gentiles enters.

Pōrōsis (G4457, hardening) is the medical word for the formation of callous or calcified tissue — not blindness but insensibility to pressure. Its qualified character (apo merous, in part) is critical: not total and not permanent. Plērōma tōn ethnōn (fullness/completion of the Gentiles) has the sense of the full quota or number: the complete Gentile ingathering that precedes Israel's restoration.
11.26: καὶ οὕτως πᾶς Ἰσραὴλ σωθήσεται — The adverb houtōs (in this way, thus) is key: it connects the mode of salvation to the process just described, not simply asserting a final result. "All Israel" is debated — whether it means the totality of ethnic Israel at a future point, or Israel as the full covenant people including Gentiles, or the full remnant elect of Israel through history. The citation follows: Isa 59:20–21 (LXX), where the Deliverer comes ek Siōn (from Zion) to turn ungodliness from Jacob. Paul adapts the LXX: the LXX has "for Zion's sake" (heneken Siōn); Paul's "from Zion" follows the MT more closely.
11.27: καὶ αὕτη αὐτοῖς ἡ παρ᾽ ἐμοῦ διαθήκη — The covenant formula from Isa 59:21 blended with Isa 27:9: "this is my covenant with them, when I take away their sins." Diathēkē (G1242, covenant) is the LXX rendering of Hebrew bĕrît. The content is the removal of sins — the new covenant language of Jer 31:33–34 standing behind this whole cluster. Paul presents Israel's future restoration as a covenantal, not merely political, act.
11.28: κατὰ μὲν τὸ εὐαγγέλιον ἐχθροὶ δι᾽ ὑμᾶς — The double kata structure draws the tension: according to the gospel they are enemies (the result of their rejection); according to election they are beloved (the result of the patriarchal covenant). Di᾽ hymas (for your sake) marks the Gentile benefit of their present enmity. Di᾽ tous pateras (because of the fathers): the patriarchal covenant is the unconditional ground of continued divine love — not current obedience or faith.
11.29: ἀμεταμέλητα γὰρ τὰ χαρίσματα καὶ ἡ κλῆσις τοῦ θεοῦ — Ametamelētos (G278, irrevocable, without afterthought) is a legal/contractual term: a grant or call that cannot be recalled by the giver. Its semantic force is not emotional (God will not regret) but volitional and covenantal (God will not reverse). Charismata (G5486, gifts) here refers to the covenant gifts of 9:4–5 (adoption, glory, covenants, law, worship, promises, patriarchs); klēsis (G2821, calling) refers to Israel's election-summons.
11.30: ὥσπερ γὰρ ὑμεῖς ποτε ἠπειθήσατε τῷ θεῷ — The past disobedience of the Gentiles (ēpeithēsate, aorist: a completed act) is now receiving mercy through Israel's disobedience. The syntax of vv.30–32 creates a set of interlocking hōsper/houtōs clauses (just as… so also) building toward the universal mercy statement of v.32. The pattern works by analogy of positions: Gentiles once disobedient → now mercy; Israel now disobedient → future mercy.
11.31: οὕτως καὶ οὗτοι νῦν ἠπείθησαν τῷ ὑμετέρῳ ἐλέει — The unusual phrase tō hymetero eleei (by your mercy) — Israel's disobedience occasions mercy to Gentiles, and that mercy to Gentiles in turn occasions mercy to Israel. The dative tō… eleei is instrumental or causal. The double nun (now) in several manuscripts is difficult: "that they too now may receive mercy now." The repetition may be textual variation or a Pauline emphasis on the urgency and present timing of the merciful plan.
11.32: συνέκλεισεν γὰρ ὁ θεὸς τοὺς πάντας εἰς ἀπείθειαν — Synekleisen (G4788, shut up together, confined) is a strong aorist: God has enclosed all in disobedience. The verb denotes imprisonment or enclosure — all are shut in to the same condition. The purpose: ἵνα τοὺς πάντας ἐλεήσῃ — so that he might show mercy to all. The double tous pantas (the all, all of them — with article) is emphatic. This does not assert universal salvation; it asserts universal need and universal availability of mercy, corresponding to the Gentile/Jew scope of chs. 1–3.
11.33: Ὦ βάθος πλούτου καὶ σοφίας καὶ γνώσεως θεοῦ — The doxology breaks from argumentative prose into exclamation. The two adjectives are stacked with verbal nouns: anexeraunēta (G419, unsearchable, not subject to investigation — the root is the same as digging/mining: the riches cannot be mined out) and anexichniastoi (G421, past tracing out — from tracking footprints: his ways leave no trackable path). The genitives sou in vv.34–35 mark quotation; the nouns krima (G2917, judgment) and hodos (G3598, way/path) encompass both his judicial decisions and his operational methods.
11.34: τίς γὰρ ἔγνω νοῦν κυρίου — Isa 40:13 (LXX). The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. Noux Kyriou — the mind of the Lord. Paul uses nous (G3563) here in its sense of the reasoning capacity, the deliberative faculty. The parallel question — who has been his counselor (symboulou, G4825)? — extends the point: God's plan is not arrived at by consultation. The same Isaiah text will appear at 1 Cor 2:16 with a different argument.
11.35: ἢ τίς προέδωκεν αὐτῷ, καὶ ἀνταποδοθήσεται αὐτῷ — Job 41:11 (LXX 41:3) or Isa 40:14. The commercial metaphor: who gave first, so that God must repay? Proedōken (G4272, give beforehand): no prior gift to God creates a debt. The future antapodothēsetai (will be repaid in return) is conditional on a prior gift — which the question establishes is impossible. Verse 35 closes the rhetorical triad with the economic impossibility of placing God under obligation.
11.36: ὅτι ἐξ αὐτοῦ καὶ δι᾽ αὐτοῦ καὶ εἰς αὐτὸν τὰ πάντα — Three prepositional phrases deploy three distinct prepositions: ek (from/out of — source), dia (through — mediation), eis (into/toward — goal). All things have God as source, instrument, and telos. The Stoic formula ex autou kai di᾽ autou kai eis auton is adapted: Paul appropriates the cosmological structure but gives it a personal subject. The doxology autō hē doxa eis tous aiōnas closes the doctrinal argument of chs. 9–11.

12.1:
παρακαλῶ οὖν ὑμᾶς, ἀδελφοί, διὰ τῶν οἰκτιρμῶν τοῦ θεοῦ — The oun (therefore) is the structural hinge linking chs. 1–11 to chs. 12–16: the entire doctrinal argument grounds the ethical appeal. Parakaleō (G3870, urge, exhort) is softer than command; the motivation clause dia tōn oiktirmōn (through the mercies of God) invokes chs. 9–11's mercy-theme. Oiktirmos (G3628, compassion) is plural here — the many acts of divine mercy form the basis of the appeal.

παραστῆσαι τὰ σώματα ὑμῶν θυσίαν ζῶσαν — The sacrificial vocabulary is deliberate: paristēmi (G3936, to present/offer, the term for presenting animals at the altar), thysia (G2378, sacrifice). But the sacrifice is zōsan (living) — not slaughtered. Hagian euareston tō theō (holy and pleasing to God) picks up the Levitical acceptability language transferred to bodily offering. τὴν λογικὴν λατρείαν ὑμῶν — Logikēn (G3050) derives from logos: what corresponds to reason, the rational/verbal offering. It is distinguished from external cultic rites (latreia, G2999, temple service) that are now replaced by embodied, rational devotion.
12.2:
μὴ συσχηματίζεσθε τῷ αἰῶνι τούτῳ — Syschēmatizō (G4964) denotes conforming to an external pattern, shaping oneself to a mold. Aión (G165) here is the present age in its temporal and moral character — the existing order with its values, ambitions, and assumptions. ἀλλὰ μεταμορφοῦσθε τῇ ἀνακαινώσει τοῦ νοός — Metamorphoō (G3339, transform) differs: it describes an inward structural change, not merely surface alteration. Anakainōsis (G342, renewal/renovation) is a hapax cognate: the noun refers to the process of making new from within. The agent is nous (G3563, mind/moral reason): the renewed faculty of discernment makes possible the proving of God's will.

εἰς τὸ δοκιμάζειν ὑμᾶς τί τὸ θέλημα τοῦ θεοῦ — Dokimazein (G1381, to test/prove/discern) is the word for assaying metal: the renewed mind is capable of accurate moral discernment. The three-fold description of God's will — to agathon kai euareston kai teleion (good, pleasing, and perfect) — is chiastic with 12:1: what is offered must be euareston (pleasing); what is discerned is the same euareston will.
12.3: μὴ ὑπερφρονεῖν παρ᾽ ὃ δεῖ φρονεῖν — Hyperphronein (to think above what one ought) and sōphronein (to think soberly) both derive from phronein (to think, to set one's mind). Paul creates a word-play: the excess of thinking highly must be restrained to the measure. The measure is κατὰ τὸ μέτρον τῆς πίστεως ὃ ἐμέρισεν ὁ θεός — the measure of faith God distributed. The metron (measure) is not self-evaluated; God distributes (emerisen, aorist) it. This grounds humility not in self-deprecation but in accurate calibration against the divine allotment.
12.4: ἐν γὰρ τῷ σώματι τῷ ἑνὶ πολλὰ μέλη ἔχομεν — The body (sōma, G4983) metaphor will be developed extensively at 1 Cor 12. Here it is compressed: one body, many members (melē, G3196), different functions (praxis, G4234, action/activity). The physical body is the analogy, not allegory: the argument depends on the observable fact of functional differentiation within organic unity.
12.5: οἱ πολλοὶ ἓν σῶμά ἐσμεν ἐν Χριστῷ — Hoi polloi (the many) becomes hen sōma (one body): plurality does not dissolve but is constituted as unity en Christō. The phrase to de kath᾽ heis allelōn melē (each one members of one another) expresses mutual belonging: the members are not merely parts of a whole but relational participants — belonging to each other.
12.6: ἔχοντες δὲ χαρίσματα κατὰ τὴν χάριν τὴν δοθεῖσαν ἡμῖν διάφορα — Charismata (G5486, gifts of grace) differ from one another: diaphora (different, various). The standard for exercise is kata tēn analogian tēs pisteōs (according to the proportion of faith): analogia (G356, proportion, ratio) is a mathematical term. Prophecy is to be exercised in proportion to one's measure of faith — the output calibrated to the input.
12.7: εἴτε διακονίαν, ἐν τῇ διακονίᾳ — The gift list is structured by eite (either… or) + noun + activity. Diakonia (G1248, service/ministry) — in service; didaskalia (G1319, teaching) — in teaching. The parallel structure in each clause: name the gift, then describe its exercise. The list is not exhaustive (cf. 1 Cor 12) but illustrative of the principle that each gift has a corresponding mode of exercise.
12.8: ὁ μεταδιδοὺς ἐν ἁπλότητι — Metadidous (the one sharing/giving, G3330): giving is a charisma here. Haplotēs (G572, simplicity/sincerity) — without ulterior motive or self-promotion. Proïstamenos (G4291, leader/one who stands before): leadership exercised en spoudē (with diligence). Eleon (G1656, mercy, compassionate act) — mercy shown en hilarotēti (G2432, hilarity/cheerfulness, the root of English "hilarious"): the etymology preserves the connection — mercy given without resentment.
12.9:
ἡ ἀγάπη ἀνυπόκριτος — The paraenesis begins with a nominative absolute, a statement about love functioning as a heading. Agapē (G26, dl=3) is the covenant-loyalty, self-giving form of love. Anypokritos (G505, without hypocrisy, unfeigned) is a single compound adjective: a- (not) + hypokrinō (to play a role, from theater). Love that is not theatrical performance.

ἀποστυγοῦντες τὸ πονηρόν, κολλώμενοι τῷ ἀγαθῷ — The participles apostygountes (abhorring, G655 — apo + stygein, to hate with a shudder) and kollōmenoi (clinging, G2853, a word for adhesion/gluing) are in sharp contrast. The strength of the language: not merely avoiding evil but detesting it; not merely pursuing good but adhering to it.
12.10: τῇ φιλαδελφίᾳ εἰς ἀλλήλους φιλόστοργοι — Philadelphia (G5360, brotherly love) and philostorgos (G5387, tenderly devoted — as family affection): Paul applies family-affection vocabulary to the community. Tē timē allēlous proēgoumenoi — in honor outstripping one another: the verb proēgeomai (G4285) is competitive — each racing to honor the other first. The competitive spirit is directed toward deferring rather than receiving honor.
12.11: τῇ σπουδῇ μὴ ὀκνηροί, τῷ πνεύματι ζέοντες — Oknēros (G3636, sluggish, lazy): not negligent in eagerness. Zeontes tō pneumati (burning/boiling in spirit, G2204): the thermal metaphor for spiritual fervor — the same word for boiling liquid. Whether pneuma here is the Holy Spirit or the human spirit is debated; the dative of sphere (in spirit) suggests it is the sphere of operation. Tō kyriō douleuontes (serving the Lord): the present participle marks ongoing service as the context of zeal.
12.12: τῇ ἐλπίδι χαίροντες, τῇ θλίψει ὑπομένοντες — Three datives of sphere, three participles: rejoicing in hope, enduring in affliction, persevering in prayer. Hypomonē (the noun root behind hypomenontes, G5278) is not passive resignation but active endurance under load. Prosedreountes tē proseuchē (G4342, continuing steadfastly in prayer): the compound pros + hedraios (firmly seated before) — holding one's position at prayer.
12.13: ταῖς χρείαις τῶν ἁγίων κοινωνοῦντες — Koinōneō (G2841, to share in/contribute to): the verb behind koinōnia. The object tais khreiais (the needs) of the saints — practical need-sharing is part of the community's identity, not an optional charity. Tēn philoxenian diōkontes (G5381, pursue hospitality): diōkō is the pursuit-word, normally used for chasing an enemy or a prize. Hospitality is to be pursued, not merely practiced when convenient.
12.14: εὐλογεῖτε τοὺς διώκοντας ὑμᾶς — The echo of Q-tradition (Matt 5:44 // Luke 6:28) is close enough that Paul may be quoting dominical teaching without identifying it as such. The present tense eulogein (to bless, speak well of) and the command not to curse (mē katarasthe) cover the same action from both sides. The ones who pursue/persecute (diōkontas) are the same word as "pursue" in v.13: the linguistic echo sharpens the irony.
12.15: χαίρειν μετὰ χαιρόντων, κλαίειν μετὰ κλαιόντων — Two infinitives used as imperatives (asyndeton — no connecting particle). Klaiein (to weep, G2799) with those who weep: Paul places this second, perhaps because it is less natural. Empathic sorrow with the grieving requires more self-transcendence than shared joy. The emotional mirroring expected of the community is comprehensive — celebration and grief.
12.16: τὸ αὐτὸ εἰς ἀλλήλους φρονοῦντες — To auto phronein (to think the same thing toward one another): the phrase does not mandate intellectual uniformity but the same orientation of mutual regard. μὴ τὰ ὑψηλὰ φρονοῦντες ἀλλὰ τοῖς ταπεινοῖς συναπαγόμενοι — Hypsēla phronein (to think high/lofty thoughts) versus tois tapeinois synapagomenoi (carried away with the lowly). Tapeinoi (G5011) may mean humble people or low social position — both senses apply. Synapagō (G4879, to be carried away with/toward) suggests being drawn into their world, not merely condescending to help them.
12.17: μηδενὶ κακὸν ἀντὶ κακοῦ ἀποδιδόντες — Anti (in exchange for): the tit-for-tat structure of retribution is the thing prohibited. Apodidōmi (G591, to give back) used of rendering what is owed: treating evil as something that creates an obligation to return evil. προνοούμενοι καλὰ ἐνώπιον πάντων ἀνθρώπων — providing for good (pronoumenoi, G4306, to think beforehand/plan for) in the sight of all people: visible integrity as a community value, not merely private virtue.
12.18: εἰ δυνατόν, τὸ ἐξ ὑμῶν, μετὰ πάντων ἀνθρώπων εἰρηνεύοντες — Two qualifications: ei dynaton (if possible — not always possible) and to ex hymōn (as far as it is from you — contingent on your own side). Paul is realistic: peace with all people is the aim, but both conditions recognize that the will of others may make it unachievable. The imperative mood of eirēneuontes applies to what lies within the community's own power.
12.19: μὴ ἑαυτοὺς ἐκδικοῦντες, ἀγαπητοί — Ekdikeō (G1556, to vindicate, avenge) — the reflexive construction: do not avenge yourselves. Agapētoi (beloved) is an unusual address in Paul's ethical sections; it signals the warmth behind the prohibition. δότε τόπον τῇ ὀργῇ — give place to the wrath: orgē with the article may indicate God's wrath specifically. The citation follows from Deut 32:35: "mine is the vengeance, I will repay." Kyrios (Lord) — in the LXX this is YHWH; Paul applies the same principle to Christian conduct.
12.20: ἐὰν πεινᾷ ὁ ἐχθρός σου, ψώμιζε αὐτόν — Prov 25:21–22 (LXX). The feeding and giving drink are the counter-action to enmity. ἄνθρακας πυρὸς σωρεύσεις ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν αὐτοῦ — Anthrakas pyros (coals of fire): the image is debated — shame (heat on the head as a sign of burning remorse), an Egyptian ritual of repentance, or simply the effect that kindness has in exposing and intensifying the enemy's guilt. The Proverbs context suggests the purpose is the enemy's transformation, not punishment: the LXX adds "and the Lord will reward you," framing the act as generosity.
12.21: μὴ νικῶ ὑπὸ τοῦ κακοῦ, ἀλλὰ νίκα ἐν τῷ ἀγαθῷ τὸ κακόν — The closing aphorism is chiastic: nikō/nikō (overcome) and kakō/kalon (evil/good). The verb nikaō (G3528, to conquer, used of military victory) frames the ethical life as agonistic: evil is a conquering force; the response is counter-conquest through good. The preposition en (in/by means of) marks the instrument: the weapon against evil is good itself.

13.1:
πᾶσα ψυχὴ ἐξουσίαις ὑπερεχούσαις ὑποτασσέσθω — Pasa psykhē (every soul) is a Hebraism for every person. Exousiai hyperekhousin — governing authorities (exousia, G1849, granted authority/competency, not raw power; hyperekhousa, G5242, excelling/surpassing — those set above). Hypotassesthō (G5293, be subject to) is the military-administrative word for placing oneself in proper order under a command structure — not blind obedience but structural recognition of rank.

οὐ γάρ ἐστιν ἐξουσία εἰ μὴ ὑπὸ θεοῦ, αἱ δὲ οὖσαι ὑπὸ θεοῦ τεταγμέναι εἰσίν — The perfect passive tetagmenai eisin (have been arranged by God and remain so): the divine ordering is presented as a standing reality. The ground of the political submission is not the virtue of rulers but the divine ordering. The argument is structural, not evaluative.
13.2: ὁ ἀντιτασσόμενος τῇ ἐξουσίᾳ τῇ τοῦ θεοῦ διαταγῇ ἀνθέστηκεν — Antitassomenos (G498, to set oneself against) versus diatagē (G1296, ordinance/ordering). The one resisting the authority resists the divine ordering (anthestēken, perfect: has taken a stand against and remains in resistance). Anthistēmi (G436) used in perfect marks a stable condition of opposition. The consequence: heautois krima lēmpsontai — they will receive judgment upon themselves.
13.3: οἱ γὰρ ἄρχοντες οὐκ εἰσὶν φόβος τῷ ἀγαθῷ ἔργῳ ἀλλὰ τῷ κακῷ — The magistrate (arkhōn, G758, ruler, chief) is described by function: terror to evil, not to good. The idealized description (rulers commend good conduct, punish bad) is not a naïve claim that all rulers behave this way, but an account of the institutional role rulers are designed to fulfill — the norm against which aberrations are measured. Theleis de mē phobeisthai tēn exousian — do you wish not to fear? Then do good.
13.4: θεοῦ γὰρ διάκονός ἐστιν σοὶ εἰς τὸ ἀγαθόν — Diakonos (G1249, servant/minister): the ruler is described as God's servant (diakonos, not doulos — a minister of a function, not a personal slave). The same term appears for the Christian minister at 15:8. τὴν μάχαιραν φορεῖ — bearing the sword (makhaira, G3162, the short sword of execution and police power). The present tense phorei (G5409, habitually wear/bear) marks the sword as permanent equipment of the office. Ekdikos eis orgēn (G1558, avenger for wrath) — the civil authority legitimately executes the divine wrath deferred by private persons in 12:19.
13.5: ὑποτάσσεσθαι οὐ μόνον διὰ τὴν ὀργὴν ἀλλὰ καὶ διὰ τὴν συνείδησιν — Two grounds for submission: fear of punishment (dia tēn orgēn) and conscience (syneidēsis, G4893). Syneidēsis is the faculty of moral self-assessment, the reflexive awareness of one's own moral status. Paul elevates submission from mere self-interest (avoiding punishment) to moral obligation arising from conscience. The second ground implies the first is insufficient motivation by itself.
13.6: διὰ τοῦτο γὰρ καὶ φόρους τελεῖτε — Phorous telein (G5411, G5055, to pay tribute/taxes): tax payment is the concrete application of the submission principle. Leitourgoi gar theou eisin — for they are ministers of God (leitourgos, G3011, a word from public service and cultic ministry). The term elevates the civil magistrate to the status of public servant in the divine economy. Eis auto touto proskarterountes (persisting in this very thing): the participle suggests the continuous, full-time character of governing service.
13.7: ἀπόδοτε πᾶσιν τὰς ὀφειλάς — Apodote (G591, give back/render): the concept of debt-payment reappears from v.8. The fourfold list — phoros (tribute, direct tax on property), telos (G5056, indirect tax/toll on goods), phobos (G5401, fear/respect), timē (G5092, honor) — exhausts the categories of civic obligation. The last two are not financial but relational. The verse marks the outer limit of civic obligation before the greater debt is named in v.8.
13.8: μηδενὶ μηδὲν ὀφείλετε εἰ μὴ τὸ ἀλλήλους ἀγαπᾶν — The wordplay on opheilē (debt, G3782) from v.7: after paying all other debts, one debt remains permanently outstanding — mutual love. The infinitive agapan (to love) is the content of the single perpetual obligation. ὁ γὰρ ἀγαπῶν τὸν ἕτερον νόμον πεπλήρωκεν — the one loving the other has fulfilled the law. The perfect peplērōken (has fulfilled and the fulfillment stands) is strong: love is not partial-law but complete-law fulfillment.
13.9: τὸ γὰρ Οὐ μοιχεύσεις, Οὐ φονεύσεις, Οὐ κλέψεις, Οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις — Paul follows a non-Masoretic order (7th, 6th, 8th, 10th commandment) — the same sequence found in some LXX manuscripts, Luke 18:20, and James 2:11. The order reflects a textual tradition Paul knew. ἀνακεφαλαιοῦται ἐν τῷ λόγῳ τούτῳ — Anakephalaioo (G346, to sum up under one head) is a rhetorical term from the summary recapitulation at the end of an argument. The commandments are not repealed but gathered under a single head: love your neighbor as yourself (Lev 19:18).
13.10: ἡ ἀγάπη τῷ πλησίον κακὸν οὐκ ἐργάζεται — Plēsion (G4139, neighbor — the one near) receives the practical definition: love works no harm to the neighbor. The negative is absolute: ouk ergazetai (does not work/produce). πλήρωμα οὖν νόμου ἡ ἀγάπη — love is the fullness (plērōma) of law. The same word used at 11:12, 25 for the fullness of Gentiles: here love brings the law to its completion, not by abolishing it but by filling it out completely.
13.11: εἰδότες τὸν καιρόν, ὅτι ὥρα ἤδη ὑμᾶς ἐξ ὕπνου ἐγερθῆναι — Kairos (G2540, the appointed time, the decisive moment) — different from chronos (clock-time). Hōra (G5610, hour) narrows the urgency: the moment for waking from sleep has arrived. Nyn gar eggyteron hēmōn hē sōtēria ē hote episteu samen — our salvation is nearer now than when we believed. Engyteron (nearer, comparative) marks progressive proximity. Sōtēria (G4991, salvation) here has an eschatological referent: the final deliverance, not the initial reception of the gospel.
13.12: ἡ νὺξ προέκοψεν, ἡ δὲ ἡμέρα ἤγγικεν — Proekopsen (G4298, has advanced, is far gone — aorist): the night has progressed toward its end. Ēngiken (G1448, has drawn near — perfect): the day stands near and remains so. The metaphor coordinates with the sleep/waking of v.11. ἀποθώμεθα οὖν τὰ ἔργα τοῦ σκότους, ἐνδυσώμεθα δὲ τὰ ὅπλα τοῦ φωτός — The clothing metaphor: apothōmetha (throw off, strip off) the works of darkness; endysōmetha (put on, clothe ourselves with) the armor of light. The military hopla (G3696, weapons/arms) rather than mere clothing heightens the agonistic framing.
13.13: ὡς ἐν ἡμέρᾳ εὐσχημόνως περιπατήσωμεν — Euschēmonōs (G2156, becomingly, with good form): the word has social connotations — behavior that conforms to recognized standards of propriety. Daylight makes conduct visible and accountable. The three paired vices — kōmois kai methais (carousing and drunkenness), koitais kai aselgeiais (sexual immorality and licentiousness), eridi kai zēlō (strife and jealousy) — pair external excess with its social destructive counterpart.
13.14: ἐνδύσασθε τὸν κύριον Ἰησοῦν Χριστόν — The positive clothing command names the garment explicitly: the Lord Jesus Christ. Endysasthe (G1746, aorist middle: clothe yourselves) — the same verb as v.12's putting on the armor of light. Here the armor is identified with a person. The command picks up the baptismal imagery of Gal 3:27 ("all who were baptized into Christ have put on Christ"). καὶ τῆς σαρκὸς πρόνοιαν μὴ ποιεῖσθε εἰς ἐπιθυμίας — Sarx (G4561) here carries the Pauline ethical sense: the fleshly nature with its desires (epithymiai). Pronoia (G4307, forethought, provision): do not make provision for the flesh's desires — do not plan for or supply what the fleshly impulses demand.

14.1: τὸν δὲ ἀσθενοῦντα τῇ πίστει προσλαμβάνεσθε — Astheneō (G770, to be weak): the weakness is in tē pistei (in faith). Here pistis (G4102, dl=3) carries a narrower sense than the soteriological pistis of chs. 3–5: it is the personal conviction that enables freedom from scrupulosity regarding food and days. The weak in faith lack this settled conviction, not saving faith. Proslambano (G4355, receive/welcome alongside) — to take someone into one's company, not merely to tolerate them. μὴ εἰς διακρίσεις διαλογισμῶν — not for judging disputed thoughts: diakrisis (G1253) is discrimination/judging; dialogismoi (G1261, calculations, reasonings) — not for the purpose of disputing over their scruples.
14.2: ὁ μὲν πιστεύει φαγεῖν πάντα — Pisteuei here means believes/is convinced (personal conviction, not saving faith). Eating all things versus eating only vegetables: the background is Jewish dietary law and the difficulty of securing ritually pure meat in Gentile contexts (cf. 1 Cor 8–10). The strong believer's freedom extends to panta (all things); the weak person eats lakhana (G3001, vegetables/herbs).
14.3: ὁ ἐσθίων τὸν μὴ ἐσθίοντα μὴ ἐξουθενείτω — Exoutheneō (G1848, to treat as nothing, despise): the eater must not despise the abstainer. ὁ δὲ μὴ ἐσθίων τὸν ἐσθίοντα μὴ κρινέτω — the abstainer must not judge (krinō, G2919) the eater. The two wrongs are different: despising (looking down from strength) and judging (condemning from scruple). Both are corrected by the same ground: ὁ θεὸς γὰρ αὐτὸν προσελάβετο — God has welcomed him.
14.4: σὺ τίς εἶ ὁ κρίνων ἀλλότριον οἰκέτην — Allotrios oiketēs (another person's household servant): to judge another person's slave is to intrude into a master-servant relationship that is not yours. Oiketēs (G3610, house-slave) rather than doulos: the domestic context emphasizes the master's exclusive prerogative of evaluation. τῷ ἰδίῳ κυρίῳ στήκει ἢ πίπτει — he stands or falls before his own master. Stēkō (G4739, stand firm) as perfect of histēmi: present standing. σταθήσεται δέ — he will stand: the divine passive asserts God's capacity to make the servant stand despite the skepticism of the judging party.
14.5: ὃς μὲν κρίνει ἡμέραν παρ᾽ ἡμέραν, ὃς δὲ κρίνει πᾶσαν ἡμέραν — The day-observance dispute: one person judges (discerns, considers, G2919) one day above another; another judges every day alike.
