The background likely includes Sabbath and feast-day observance. ἕκαστος ἐν τῷ ἰδίῳ νοῒ πληροφορείσθω — each person be fully persuaded in their own mind (plērophoreō, G4135, to fully convince, fill with certainty). Nous (G3563) is the moral reasoning faculty. The responsibility for conviction rests with each individual before God, not with the community's consensus.
14.6: ὁ φρονῶν τὴν ἡμέραν κυρίῳ φρονεῖ — Phronō (G5426, to think, to regard with mindful attention) — the one observing the day observes it to the Lord. The parallel construction: eating/abstaining, both directed to the Lord with thanksgiving (eukharisteō, G2168). The criterion for both is the same: orientation toward the Lord and giving of thanks. The content of the practice differs; the Lord-directedness does not.
14.7: οὐδεὶς γὰρ ἡμῶν ἑαυτῷ ζῇ καὶ οὐδεὶς ἑαυτῷ ἀποθνῄσκει — The principle underlying the Lord-orientation of v.6: no one lives to themselves, no one dies to themselves. Heautō (dative of benefit — for himself alone): life and death are not self-enclosed transactions. The verse is a general anthropological claim before the Christological ground is given in vv.8–9.
14.8: ἐάν τε γὰρ ζῶμεν, τῷ κυρίῳ ζῶμεν, ἐάν τε ἀποθνῄσκωμεν, τῷ κυρίῳ ἀποθνῄσκομεν — The dative tō kyriō in all four clauses: to the Lord we live, to the Lord we die. Eante (whether) creates the exhaustive disjunction. ἐάν τε οὖν ζῶμεν ἐάν τε ἀποθνῄσκωμεν, τοῦ κυρίου ἐσμέν — the conclusion: we are the Lord's. The genitive tou kyriou marks ownership/belonging — existence in either state is possession by the Lord.
14.9: εἰς τοῦτο γὰρ Χριστὸς ἀπέθανεν καὶ ἔζησεν — The aorist-then-aorist: apethanen (died) and ezēsen (lived/came to life — a constative aorist for the resurrection). The purpose: ἵνα καὶ νεκρῶν καὶ ζώντων κυριεύσῃ — so that he might be Lord of both dead and living. The scope of Christ's lordship is total: death and resurrection together establish his dominion over all human states. The present-tense ethical claim of ch. 14 is grounded in this Christological fact.
14.10: σὺ δὲ τί κρίνεις τὸν ἀδελφόν σου; ἢ καὶ σὺ τί ἐξουθενεῖς τὸν ἀδελφόν σου — Two questions — one to the judger, one to the despiser (reversing the order of v.3 to address each party directly). Both actions are delegitimized by the ground that follows: πάντες γὰρ παραστησόμεθα τῷ βήματι τοῦ θεοῦ — all of us will stand before God's judgment seat (bēma, G968, the raised platform of judgment). The plurality and the universality (pantes) undercut the presumption of any individual's pre-judgment of another.
14.11: γέγραπται γάρ — The citation is from Isa 45:23 (LXX), here blended with Isa 49:18. The perfect gegraptai (it stands written): the scriptural authority is invoked as permanent. Zō egō, legei kyrios — the divine oath formula ("As I live, says the Lord"): God's own life is the pledge. ἐμοὶ κάμψει πᾶν γόνυ καὶ πᾶσα γλῶσσα ἐξομολογήσεται τῷ θεῷ — every knee will bow, every tongue will confess. At Phil 2:10–11 Paul applies the same text to Jesus; here it grounds accountability to God as judge. The application is consistent: the one before whom every knee bows is the one before whose judgment seat all will stand.
14.12: ἄρα οὖν ἕκαστος ἡμῶν περὶ ἑαυτοῦ λόγον δώσει τῷ θεῷ — Ara oun (therefore then): the logical conclusion from vv.10–11. Logos dōsei (will give account): the forensic idiom — rendering an accounting before a court. Peri heautou (about himself): the account is individual and personal. The implication: since each person must give account for himself, judging another's account in advance is a category error — it presumes standing the judge does not possess.
14.13: μηκέτι οὖν ἀλλήλους κρίνωμεν — The hortatory subjunctive shifts to communal resolution: let us no longer judge one another. ἀλλὰ τοῦτο κρίνατε μᾶλλον — rather, decide (krinate, aorist imperative of krinō) this: a playful inversion of the prohibition, using the same verb. τὸ μὴ τιθέναι πρόσκομμα τῷ ἀδελφῷ ἢ σκάνδαλον — not to place a stumbling-block (proskomma, G4348) or a trap (skandalon, G4625, the trigger-stick of an animal trap, then metaphorically an occasion for moral failure).
14.14: οἶδα καὶ πέπεισμαι ἐν κυρίῳ Ἰησοῦ ὅτι οὐδὲν κοινὸν δι᾽ ἑαυτοῦ — Oida (know) + pepeismai (perfect passive of peithō, to persuade — am fully persuaded, G3982): Paul asserts personal conviction at the highest level. Koinos (G2839, common/unclean) — in the ritual purity sense. The principle: nothing is unclean by its own nature (di᾽ heautou). εἰ μὴ τῷ λογιζομένῳ τι κοινὸν εἶναι, ἐκείνῳ κοινόν — but for the one who considers it unclean, it is unclean. Logizomenō (G3049, to reckon, count, consider): the act of personal moral reckoning determines the category for that person. The principle respects the primacy of conscience.
14.15: εἰ γὰρ διὰ βρῶμα ὁ ἀδελφός σου λυπεῖται — Brōma (G1033, food) is the material object; lypeō (G3076, to grieve, distress) describes the effect on the brother. οὐκέτι κατὰ ἀγάπην περιπατεῖς — you are no longer walking according to love: kata agapēn (according to love) is the governing principle of conduct. μὴ τῷ βρώματί σου ἐκεῖνον ἀπόλλυε ὑπὲρ οὗ Χριστὸς ἀπέθανεν — the escalation is stark: destroying (apollye, G622) someone for whom Christ died — over food. The relative clause hyper hou Christos apethanen (for whom Christ died) grounds the prohibition in the infinite value of the person.
14.16: μὴ βλασφημείσθω οὖν ὑμῶν τὸ ἀγαθόν — Blasphēmeitō (G987, spoken of as evil): the freedom of the strong, which is genuinely good (to agathon), must not become an occasion for the outsider's blasphemy. The "good" being blasphemed is the liberty in Christ; the behavior of the strong, if exercised without regard for the weak, turns a gift into a scandal.
14.17: οὐ γάρ ἐστιν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ βρῶσις καὶ πόσις — Basileia tou theou (kingdom of God): the governing rule of God in its present experiential expression. The kingdom's substance is not eating and drinking (brōsis kai posis) — not dietary and ritual practice. ἀλλὰ δικαιοσύνη καὶ εἰρήνη καὶ χαρὰ ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ — Dikaiosynē (G1343, dl=4) here is behavioral/moral uprightness within the community, not the forensic justification of chs. 1–8; eirēnē (peace) is communal harmony; khara (joy) is the Spirit-produced delight. En pneumati hagiō (in the Holy Spirit): the Spirit is the sphere in which these qualities exist and the agent who produces them.
14.18: ὁ γὰρ ἐν τούτῳ δουλεύων τῷ Χριστῷ εὐάρεστος τῷ θεῷ καὶ δόκιμος τοῖς ἀνθρώποις — En toutō douleuōn (serving in this — in righteousness, peace, and joy): the participle picks up the three nouns of v.17. Euarestos tō theō (pleasing to God, G2101) — the same word as 12:1's "acceptable/pleasing to God." Dokimos tois anthrōpois (G1384, approved by people — having passed the test): the life lived in the Spirit's fruits wins the approval of the human community as well as God.
14.19: ἄρα οὖν τὰ τῆς εἰρήνης διώκωμεν — Ara oun + hortatory subjunctive: the conclusion drives to action. Diōkō (G1377, pursue, chase): the competitive-pursuit word recurs from 12:13. Ta tēs eirēnēs (the things of peace) and ta tēs oikodomēs (the things of building up/edification, G3619): two goal-objects to pursue. Oikodomē (building, edifice) used of community formation is a Pauline technical term for the constructive growth of the community.
14.20: μὴ ἕνεκεν βρώματος κατάλυε τὸ ἔργον τοῦ θεοῦ — Katalyō (G2647, to tear down, demolish — the building-metaphor in reverse). The work of God (the community, the weak believer) is not to be demolished for food. πάντα μὲν καθαρά — all things are clean (the principle Paul agrees with). ἀλλὰ κακὸν τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ τῷ διὰ προσκόμματος ἐσθίοντι — but it is evil for the person who eats through a stumbling-block: the manner of eating that creates the stumbling-block for another is the evil. The freedom is real; the exercise that harms another is not.
14.21: καλὸν τὸ μὴ φαγεῖν κρέα μηδὲ πιεῖν οἶνον μηδὲ ἐν ᾧ ὁ ἀδελφός σου προσκόπτει — The infinitives are articular (to mē phagein): the good thing is the concrete action of not eating meat, not drinking wine, not doing anything by which the brother stumbles. The list is practical — wine as well as meat were relevant to the Roman context (Diaspora Jewish practice varied on wine at Gentile tables). The conclusion states the norm positively: abstinence, when it prevents a sibling's fall, is good.
14.22: σὺ πίστιν ἣν ἔχεις κατὰ σεαυτὸν ἔχε ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ — Pistin hēn ekheis (the faith/conviction you have): the strong person's liberty of conscience is a possession to be kept (kata seauton, between yourself and God, not paraded before the community). μακάριος ὁ μὴ κρίνων ἑαυτὸν ἐν ᾧ δοκιμάζει — blessed is the one who does not judge himself in what he approves: dokimazō (G1381, to test/approve). The blessing is for the person whose conscience (pistis/conviction) is settled enough that self-condemnation does not arise.
14.23: ὁ δὲ διακρινόμενος ἐὰν φάγῃ κατακέκριται — Diakrinomenos (G1252, the one who doubts/wavers, hesitates between options): the wavering person who eats against their own scruple is condemned (katakrinetai, G2632, perfect passive: has been condemned and the condemnation stands). The condemnation is self-condemnation through conscience: ὅτι οὐκ ἐκ πίστεως — because not from faith/conviction. πᾶν δὲ ὃ οὐκ ἐκ πίστεως ἁμαρτία ἐστίν — everything not from faith is sin. Here pistis means the personal settled conviction that makes an action conscientiously permissible for the person doing it. The maxim is not about saving faith but about the relationship between conscience and action: acting against one's own conscience is sin, regardless of the objective permissibility of the act.

4.1: τὸν προπάτορα ἡμῶν κατὰ σάρκα (ton propatora hēmōn kata sarka) — "our forefather according to the flesh." The kata sarka qualification anticipates Paul's argument: Abraham is relevant as forefather according to the flesh only as a starting point; his real significance is as father of faith (vv11-12). The shift from fleshly descent to faith-descent is the letter's central move for defining who belongs to the people of God.
4.2: ἔχει καύχημα ἀλλ᾽ οὐ πρὸς θεόν (echei kauchēma all' ou pros theon) — "he has a boast, but not before God." Kauchēma (boast, ground of pride) — Paul grants the hypothetical: if Abraham were justified by works, he would have a legitimate ground of pride before other humans. But the divine court operates differently; what impresses human evaluators does not constitute righteousness before God, whose standard is being-without-boast (3:27).
4.3: ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην (elogisthē autō eis dikaiosynēn) — "it was credited to him as righteousness." The aorist passive of logizomai: a single completed act of divine reckoning. The subject is "faith" (Abraham's trust in God's promise); the object reckoned is "righteousness" — the standing before God that the faith-act receives as its divine valuation. The verb will be repeated eight times in ch4, building a sustained argument from the single Genesis text (Gen 15:6).
4.4: τὸ μισθὸς οὐ λογίζεται κατὰ χάριν ἀλλὰ κατὰ ὀφείλημα (to misthos ou logizetai kata charin alla kata opheilēma) — "the wage is not credited according to grace but according to what is owed." Opheilēma (debt, what is owed) — the wage-worker analogy establishes the contrast: wages are debt-obligations, not gifts. If justification were by works, God would be the debtor; but God is no one's debtor, making justification-by-works a category error about the nature of the divine-human relationship.
4.5: τῷ δὲ μὴ ἐργαζομένῳ πιστεύοντι δὲ ἐπὶ τὸν δικαιοῦντα τὸν ἀσεβῆ (tō de mē ergazomenō pisteuonti de epi ton dikaiounta ton asebē) — "but to the one who does not work but believes in the one who justifies the ungodly." Ton asebē (the ungodly one) is the stunning element: God justifies the ungodly, not the merely-imperfect or the almost-righteous. The one who has no works to offer is precisely the candidate for grace-reckoning. Paul's description of God as "the one who justifies the ungodly" directly inverts Exod 23:7 ("do not acquit the guilty").
4.6: μακαρισμὸν (makarismon) — "blessedness," the state of being blessed — the same root as the Beatitudes (makarios). David's declaration of blessedness in Ps 32:1-2 becomes Paul's second OT witness (after Gen 15:6) that right-standing before God is received, not earned. The Psalter's own voice confirms the Genesis principle.
4.7: ἀφέθησαν... ἐπεκαλύφθησαν (aphethēsan... epekaluphthēsan) — "have been forgiven... have been covered" — both aorist passives; the divine act of forgiveness is completed and stands. Aphiēmi (to send away, release) and epikaryptō (to cover over) are two images for the same reality: the debt is cancelled and the evidence is hidden. LXX Ps 32:1 uses these verbs; Paul imports them verbatim.
4.8: οὗ οὐ μὴ λογίσηται κύριος ἁμαρτίαν (hou ou mē logisētai kyrios hamartian) — "to whom the Lord will never reckon sin" — the double negative ou mē with the subjunctive is emphatic negation: under no circumstances, not ever. The future subjunctive extends the non-reckoning into perpetuity. The same reckoning-verb that credits righteousness positively here refuses to credit sin negatively — both sides of the forensic ledger are covered by grace.
4.9: ἐν περιτομῇ ὄντι ἢ καὶ ἐν ἀκροβυστίᾳ (en peritomē onti ē kai en akrobystia) — "to the circumcised or also to the uncircumcised?" Paul now asks when Abraham received the blessing — before or after circumcision. The temporal argument from Genesis: Gen 15:6 (faith credited) precedes Gen 17 (circumcision instituted) by approximately 14 years, making Abraham's justification happen in uncircumcision.
4.10: οὐκ ἐν περιτομῇ ἀλλ᾽ ἐν ἀκροβυστίᾳ — "not in circumcision but in uncircumcision" — the historical sequence is Paul's argument: the faith-reckoning happened when Abraham was still an uncircumcised Gentile. The implication is that faith-righteousness is structurally prior to covenant-rite, making it available to all who believe regardless of circumcision-status.
4.11: σφραγῖδα τῆς δικαιοσύνης τῆς πίστεως (sphragida tēs dikaiosynēs tēs pisteōs) — "a seal of the righteousness of the faith" — sphragis (seal) is the authentication mark that certifies a document's validity. Circumcision is the external seal that certifies the prior righteousness-by-faith; it does not produce the righteousness but certifies its existence. This makes circumcision meaningful but non-essential for the righteousness itself.
4.12: τοῖς στοιχοῦσιν τοῖς ἴχνεσιν τῆς ἐν ἀκροβυστίᾳ πίστεως (tois stoichousin tois ichnesin tēs en akrobystia pisteōs) — "who follow in the footsteps of the faith that our father Abraham had while still uncircumcised." Stoicheō (to march in line, follow in order) + ichnesin (footprints, tracks) — a vivid image of following someone's literal footsteps. Those who retrace Abraham's faith-path (not his circumcision-path) become his offspring.
4.13: κληρονόμον αὐτὸν εἶναι κόσμου (klēronomon auton einai kosmou) — "that he would be heir of the world" — klēronomos (heir, one who receives the inheritance by lot) — the promise to Abraham (land, seed, blessing) is here universalized to "the world," which Paul reads as the eschatological new-creation inheritance. The local promise to Canaan becomes the cosmic promise of the new earth; Paul reads Genesis through the lens of the prophets who expanded the Abrahamic promise to universal scope.
4.14: εἰ γὰρ οἱ ἐκ νόμου κληρονόμοι (ei gar hoi ek nomou klēronomoi) — "if those who are of the law are heirs" — the conditional hypothetical: if inheritance were through law-keeping, then faith would be emptied (kekenōtai, perfect passive — "has been emptied/rendered void") and the promise would be nullified (katērgētai — "rendered inoperative"). The law cannot be the mechanism of inheritance because it cannot do what it demands (3:19-20).
4.15: ὁ γὰρ νόμος ὀργὴν κατεργάζεται (ho gar nomos orgēn katergazetai) — "for the law brings about wrath" — the present active: law's operation produces wrath because it defines the standard that condemns its own transgressors. οὗ δὲ οὐκ ἔστιν νόμος, οὐδὲ παράβασις — "where there is no law, there is no transgression either" — parabasis (deliberate stepping-past) requires a known boundary; without the law, moral failure is hamartia (missing the mark) but not parabasis (knowing violation).
4.16: ἐκ πίστεως, ἵνα κατὰ χάριν (ek pisteōs, hina kata charin) — "from faith, so that according to grace" — the causal chain: faith-basis → grace-character → promise-security. The logic is: only a grace-based promise can be universally reliable, because performance-based promises fail when performance fails. βεβαίαν εἶναι τὴν ἐπαγγελίαν παντὶ τῷ σπέρματι — "that the promise might be secure to all the offspring" — bebaios (firm, guaranteed, bankable) — a commercial-legal term for a guaranteed transaction; the promise is reliable precisely because it depends on God's faithfulness, not human performance.
4.17: τοῦ ζωοποιοῦντος τοὺς νεκροὺς καὶ καλοῦντος τὰ μὴ ὄντα ὡς ὄντα (tou zōopoiountos tous nekrous kai kalountos ta mē onta hōs onta) — "the one who gives life to the dead and calls things that are not as though they are." Two divine attributes grounding Abraham's faith: (1) zōopoieō (give life) — applicable both to Sarah's womb and to the resurrection; (2) the creative calling of non-being into being (ta mē onta hōs onta) — the language of creatio ex nihilo. The God Abraham trusted is the Creator and Resurrector simultaneously.
4.18: παρ᾽ ἐλπίδα ἐπ᾽ ἐλπίδι (par' elpida ep' elpidi) — "against hope, in hope." The paradoxical phrase is precisely constructed: par' elpida (contrary to visible/calculable expectation) and ep' elpidi (grounded-on-hope = the divine promise). Abraham's faith is rational at the divine level while irrational at the human level; it trusts the God who makes promises rather than the circumstances that deny their possibility.
4.19: μὴ ἀσθενήσας τῇ πίστει (mē asthenēsas tē pistei) — "not weakening in faith" — the aorist participle: he did not at the decisive moment collapse in unbelief. κατενόησεν τὸ ἑαυτοῦ σῶμα ἤδη νενεκρωμένον (katenovēsen to heautou sōma ēdē nenekrōmenon) — "he considered his own body, already deadened" — nenekrōmenon: perfect passive participle of nekroō (to put to death, make corpse-like). Abraham saw the biological reality clearly; his faith was not blind optimism but clear-eyed trust in the promise-maker.
4.20: εἰς δὲ τὴν ἐπαγγελίαν τοῦ θεοῦ οὐ διεκρίθη τῇ ἀπιστίᾳ ἀλλ᾽ ἐνεδυναμώθη τῇ πίστει — "but regarding the promise of God he did not waver in unbelief but was strengthened in faith." Diakrinomai (to be divided, waver, doubt) — the passive implies an external force that Abraham did not yield to. Enedynamoō (to be empowered, strengthened) — the passive equally implies a divine empowerment; faith itself is a gift that enables trust in the promise. δοὺς δόξαν τῷ θεῷ — "giving glory to God" — glory-giving through faith rather than through performance is Paul's definition of true worship.
4.21: πεπληροφορημένος (peplērophorēmenos) — perfect passive participle of plērophoreō (to be fully persuaded, carried to fullness of conviction). The completeness of Abraham's persuasion is emphasized by the plēro- prefix and the perfect tense: he arrived at and remained in full conviction. The object: the divine ability to do what he promised (ποιῆσαι, hoper epēngeltai, dynatos ēstin).
4.22: διὸ καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην — "therefore it was also credited to him as righteousness" — the dio kai (therefore also) marks the logical consequence: the faith just described in vv17-21 is the faith that Gen 15:6 credits as righteousness. Paul's description of Abraham's faith is a theological unpacking of the Genesis statement, not a separate claim.
4.23: οὐκ ἐγράφη δὲ δι᾽ αὐτὸν μόνον (ouk egraphē de di' auton monon) — "but it was not written for his sake only" — the passive egraphē (it was written) is a divine passive: God wrote the Scripture for all its future readers, not merely as a record of Abraham's biography. The hermeneutical principle: OT narrative has a prospective, not merely retrospective, function.
4.24: τοῖς πιστεύουσιν ἐπὶ τὸν ἐγείραντα Ἰησοῦν τὸν κύριον ἡμῶν ἐκ νεκρῶν — "for those who believe in the one who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead." The resurrection of Christ is the definitive divine act that constitutes the content of the present-day faith that receives the same reckoning Abraham received. Believing in the resurrection-God is the NT form of Abraham's believing in the promise-God.
4.25: παρεδόθη διὰ τὰ παραπτώματα ἡμῶν καὶ ἠγέρθη διὰ τὴν δικαίωσιν ἡμῶν — "he was handed over on account of our trespasses and raised on account of our justification." The chiasm (death → trespasses; resurrection → justification) pairs the two events with their respective grounds. Egēgertai in other contexts would be perfect (4:25 uses aorist here: ēgerthē); the aorist marks the historical event. Dia with accusative can mean "on account of/for the sake of" or "because of": both meanings are simultaneously true — Christ raised because justification is accomplished and in order that justification is applied.

5.1: εἰρήνην ἔχομεν πρὸς τὸν θεόν (eirēnēn echomen pros ton theon) — "we have peace with God." The indicative echomen (we have) versus the subjunctive schōmen (let us have) is one of the most debated textual variants in the NT. External evidence slightly favors the subjunctive (Codices Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, Alexandrinus), but the indicative makes better logical sense in context: Paul has just argued for justification by faith and now states its consequences. Eirēnē here is the OT shalom — not merely absence of conflict but positive relational wholeness with God.
5.2:
τὴν προσαγωγὴν ἐσχήκαμεν (tēn prosagōgēn eschēkamen) — "we have gained access" — perfect active of echō: a past gaining with continuing possession. Prosagōgē (access, approach) is a technical term from the royal court: the formal introduction that grants audience with the king; or from the temple: the approach to the divine presence. Through Christ the believer has been formally introduced into the divine presence and maintains ongoing access.

ἐν ᾗ ἑστήκαμεν (en hē hestēkamen) — "in which we stand" — perfect active of histēmi: having taken a position and remaining in it. Grace is not merely the door through which one passes but the sphere in which one permanently stands.
5.3: καυχώμεθα ἐν ταῖς θλίψεσιν (kauchōmetha en tais thlipsesin) — "we boast in our sufferings" — the same verb (kauchaomai) used for the boasting that faith excludes (3:27); now Paul redirects it toward sufferings. Thlipsis (pressure, affliction, tribulation) — from thlibō (to press, squeeze); concrete physical-social suffering, not merely inconvenience. εἰδότες ὅτι (eidotes hoti) — "knowing that" — the boasting is knowledge-based, not masochistic; the chain that follows (suffering → endurance → character → hope) is the rationale.
5.4: ἡ δὲ ὑπομονὴ δοκιμήν (hē de hypomonē dokimēn) — "and endurance produces character" — hypomonē (endurance, remaining-under) and dokimē (tested character, that which has passed the test). The metallurgical testing image: the pressured metal that passes the test becomes certified as genuine. Suffering → tested endurance → certified character is the logic.
5.5: οὐ καταισχύνει (ou kataischynei) — "does not put to shame/disappoint" — present active: the hope is continuously non-disappointing. The Roman context makes this pointed: hope in crucified criminals typically did put one to shame. Paul's claim is that this particular hope is vindicated by the Spirit's testimony. ἐκκέχυται (ekkechytai) — "has been poured out" — perfect passive: the divine love was poured and remains poured, a completed and abiding reality rather than a past event now concluded.
5.6: ἔτι γὰρ Χριστὸς ὄντων ἡμῶν ἀσθενῶν κατὰ καιρὸν ὑπὲρ ἀσεβῶν ἀπέθανεν — "for while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly." Kata kairon (at the right moment, the appointed time) — kairos is qualitative time (significant moment) rather than chronological time (chronos); the timing of Christ's death is providentially exact. Asebōn (for the ungodly) — the same word as 4:5; Christ died for exactly those whom God justifies.
5.7: μόλις γὰρ ὑπὲρ δικαίου τις ἀποθανεῖται — "for one would scarcely die for a righteous person" — the moral gradient Paul establishes: hardly anyone dies for the righteous (dikaios); perhaps someone dares to die for the genuinely good (agathos). The distinction between the merely-law-keeping righteous and the lovable-good represents the upper limit of human self-sacrifice.
5.8: συνίστησιν δὲ τὴν ἑαυτοῦ ἀγάπην εἰς ἡμᾶς ὁ θεός (synistēsin de tēn heautou agapēn eis hēmas ho theos) — "but God demonstrates his own love toward us." Synistēmi (to establish, demonstrate, commend, prove) — more than display; the cross is a proof, an argument, a demonstration of the character of divine love. The emphasis on "his own" (heautou) and "toward us" (eis hēmas) makes the love personal and directed.
5.9: πολλῷ οὖν μᾶλλον (pollō oun mallon) — "much more therefore" — the a-fortiori argument Paul will use repeatedly in vv9-11: if X (the greater) has been accomplished, how much more certainly Y (the lesser) will follow. Justification by Christ's blood is the greater (costly, accomplished while we were sinners); salvation from wrath is the lesser (what follows from the accomplished justification).
5.10: εἰ γὰρ ἐχθροὶ ὄντες κατηλλάγημεν τῷ θεῷ (ei gar echthroi ontes katēllagēmen tō theō) — "for if while being enemies we were reconciled to God." Echthroi ontes — present participle: enemies as a present active state. Katēllagēmen — aorist passive: the reconciliation was accomplished from God's side, not worked up from ours. The a-fortiori: if reconciliation happened while we were enemies, how much more will salvation be certain now that we are friends. διὰ τοῦ θανάτου τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ — "through the death of his Son" — reconciliation grounds in the most extreme possible divine act.
5.11: καυχώμενοι ἐν τῷ θεῷ (kauchōmenoi en tō theō) — "boasting in God" — the third boasting in the chapter (v2: in hope; v3: in sufferings; now in God himself). The movement is: from circumstances → to suffering → to the God who is the ground of all three. Katallagē (reconciliation) — from allassō (to exchange); reconciliation as exchange: God takes our enmity and gives his friendship. The exchange theology Paul is building toward (5:12-21) begins here.
5.12: δι᾽ ἑνὸς ἀνθρώπου ἡ ἁμαρτία εἰς τὸν κόσμον εἰσῆλθεν — "through one man sin entered the world." The historical singular act: Adam's disobedience as the point of entry. ἐφ᾽ ᾧ πάντες ἥμαρτον (eph' hō pantes hēmarton) — the most debated phrase in the passage. Eph' hō can mean (a) "because of whom" (relative pronoun — in whom = in Adam all sinned, Augustinian reading), (b) "in that" or "because" (conjunction — because all individually sinned, Eastern reading). The causal reading is grammatically more natural in Koine Greek; Paul probably means "because all individually sinned" while also holding the Adamic solidarity of vv15-21.
5.13: ἄχρι γὰρ νόμου ἁμαρτία ἦν ἐν κόσμῳ (achri gar nomou hamartia ēn en kosmō) — "for until the law sin was in the world." Paul distinguishes sin (hamartia, the reality) from transgression (parabasis, knowing violation of a known command): sin existed between Adam and Moses but could not be formally charged as parabasis without a codified law.
5.14: τύπος τοῦ μέλλοντος (typos tou mellontos) — "a type/pattern of the one to come." Typos (stamp, impression, figure) — the word from which "typology" derives; Adam is the structural pattern whose negative is filled positively by Christ. The type/antitype relationship is explicit here: Adam's one-act-of-disobedience-with-universal-consequence finds its counter in Christ's one-act-of-obedience-with-universal-consequence (v18-19).
5.15: οὐχ ὡς τὸ παράπτωμα, οὕτως καὶ τὸ χάρισμα (ouch hōs to paraptōma, houtōs kai to charisma) — "but the gift is not like the trespass." Paraptōma (a falling-beside, trespass, deviation) and charisma (grace-gift). The dissimilarity Paul will emphasize: the trespass and the gift operate by different logic — one produces death through one; the other overflows life through many. The pollō mallon (much more) again: if the trespass accomplished much damage, the grace accomplishes overwhelmingly more.
5.16: κρίμα μὲν γὰρ ἐξ ἑνὸς εἰς κατάκριμα, τὸ δὲ χάρισμα ἐκ πολλῶν παραπτωμάτων εἰς δικαίωμα — "judgment from one sin brought condemnation, but the gift from many trespasses brought justification." The asymmetry: the condemnation came from one trespass; the justification covers many trespasses. Katakrima (condemnation, the sentence itself) versus dikaiōma (justification, righteous decree/act). Grace absorbs what law multiplies.
5.17: ἐν ζωῇ βασιλεύσουσιν (en zōē basileusousin) — "they will reign in life" — the eschatological reversal of Adam's condition: not merely restored to pre-fall status but elevated to reigning. The future tense (basileusousin) is both already-realized (in the Spirit's firstfruits) and still-anticipated (at the resurrection). Those who receive the abundance of grace become kings where Adam became a slave to death.
5.18: δι᾽ ἑνὸς δικαιώματος (di' henos dikaiōmatos) — "through one righteous act" — dikaiōma here means the single act of obedience (Christ's death as fulfillment of righteousness) rather than the legal verdict. The parallel with v16's "one trespass" is exact. εἰς δικαίωσιν ζωῆς — "resulting in justification of life" — the genitive zōēs (life) describes the character of the justification: it is a justification that produces life rather than merely legal standing.
5.19: διὰ τῆς ὑπακοῆς τοῦ ἑνός (dia tēs hypakoēs tou henos) — "through the obedience of the one." The contrast with "the one man's disobedience" is exact. Hypakoē (obedience, literally: hearing-under) — the obedient life of Christ including but not limited to his death. κατασταθήσονται δίκαιοι (katastathēsontai dikaioi) — "will be constituted righteous" — future passive; the eschatological establishment of righteousness in the many, not merely its forensic declaration.
5.20: νόμος δὲ παρεισῆλθεν (nomos de pareisēlthen) — "the law came in alongside" — pareiserchomai (to come in beside, slip in alongside) — the verb suggests the law's role as secondary, not primary: it entered the scene that had already been established by Adam's sin and the promise to Abraham. ἵνα πλεονάσῃ τὸ παράπτωμα — "so that the trespass might increase" — the purpose clause is deliberately provocative: the law's purpose includes the multiplication of trespass (clarifying, specifying, and thus increasing the catalog of violations). But: οὗ δὲ ἐπλεόνασεν ἡ ἁμαρτία, ὑπερεπερίσσευσεν ἡ χάρις — "where sin increased, grace superabounded." Hyperperisseuō (to overflow beyond all measure) — the hyper- prefix intensifies the already-intense perisseuō; grace exceeds sin by a qualitative order of magnitude, not merely a quantitative surplus.
5.21: βασιλεύσῃ ἡ χάρις διὰ δικαιοσύνης εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον (basileusē hē charis dia dikaiosynēs eis zōēn aiōnion) — "grace might reign through righteousness unto eternal life." Grace is personified as the royal power that replaces sin's reign (v21a); its instrument is righteousness and its destination is eternal life. The political metaphor (reign, kingship) that has governed vv12-21 reaches its conclusion: the governance of reality has changed sovereigns through Jesus Christ.

6.1: ἐπιμένωμεν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ἵνα ἡ χάρις πλεονάσῃ (epimenōmen tē hamartia hina hē charis pleonasē) — "shall we continue in sin so that grace may increase?" The logical inference from 5:20 stated as the objection Paul anticipated. Epimenō (to remain upon, persist in) — the deliberate, continued choice to remain in sin, not accidental failure. The mē genoito (certainly not!) that follows does not argue against the inference; it demonstrates the absurdity of what the inference requires one to overlook.
6.2: οἵτινες ἀπεθάνομεν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ (hoitines apethanomen tē hamartia) — "we who died to sin" — aorist active: a completed death. The relative clause hoitines (we who are such people that) identifies the believers by this death-event. πῶς ἔτι ζήσομεν ἐν αὐτῇ — "how shall we still live in it?" — the rhetorical question points to the ontological impossibility: the dead person cannot continue in the life they died out of.
6.3: ἐβαπτίσθημεν εἰς Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν (ebaptisthēmen eis Christon Iēsoun) — "were baptized into Christ Jesus" — the eis (into) is significant: baptism is the act of going-into-Christ, of union with his person. εἰς τὸν θάνατον αὐτοῦ ἐβαπτίσθημεν — "baptized into his death" — the union is specifically with the death-event; baptism does not merely commemorate Christ's death but unites the baptized person with it.
6.4: συνετάφημεν οὖν αὐτῷ διὰ τοῦ βαπτίσματος (synetaphēmen oun autō dia tou baptismatos) — "we were buried therefore with him through baptism" — syntaptō (to bury together with) — a compound verb of solidarity: not just that Christ was buried but that we were buried with him. ἐν καινότητι ζωῆς (en kainotēti zōēs) — "in newness of life" — kainotēs (newness in kind, qualitative novelty) as opposed to neos (new in age/time); the resurrection life is not the old life renewed but an ontologically new mode of existence.
6.5: σύμφυτοι γεγόναμεν τῷ ὁμοιώματι τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ (symphytoi gegonamen tō homoiōmati tou thanatou autou) — "we have been united with the likeness of his death." Symphytos (grown-together-with, organically joined) — a horticultural term (cf. grafting): the believer is not merely alongside Christ's death as a moral example but organically united to it. The perfect gegonamen (we have become and remain) indicates permanent organic union.
6.6: ὁ παλαιὸς ἡμῶν ἄνθρωπος συνεσταυρώθη (ho palaios hēmōn anthrōpos synestaurōthē) — "our old man was crucified together with him" — palaios anthrōpos (old humanity) — not the body but the previous Adam-shaped mode of existence. Systauroō (to crucify together with) — the compound form of crucifixion: co-crucifixion, union in the death event. ἵνα καταργηθῇ τὸ σῶμα τῆς ἁμαρτίας — "so that the body of sin might be rendered inoperative" — katargeō again: not destroyed but rendered powerless, inactivated.
6.7: ὁ γὰρ ἀποθανὼν δεδικαίωται ἀπὸ τῆς ἁμαρτίας (ho gar apothanōn dedikaiōtai apo tēs hamartias) — "for the one who has died has been justified from sin." The perfect passive dedikaiōtai: has been and remains acquitted from sin's claims. Death severs all legal claims; the dead person owes no further obligations to the old master. This principle Paul applies: the believer who has died with Christ is no longer subject to sin's legal authority.
6.8: πιστεύομεν ὅτι καὶ συζήσομεν αὐτῷ (pisteuomen hoti kai syzēsomen autō) — "we believe that we will also live together with him." The future tense syzēsomen (we will co-live) points to the future resurrection as the completion of the union initiated in baptism; but the present tense pisteuomen (we are believing) grounds the future hope in present faith rather than future achievement.
6.9: Χριστὸς ἐγερθεὶς ἐκ νεκρῶν οὐκέτι ἀποθνῄσκει — "Christ, having been raised from the dead, no longer dies." Egertheisidēs (aorist passive participle: having been raised) describes the historical event; apothnēskei (present tense: dies) the ongoing state — he no longer dies. θάνατος αὐτοῦ οὐκέτι κυριεύει — "death no longer lords it over him" — kyrieuō (to be lord, to exercise dominion) — the resurrection terminates death's lordship over Christ. Believers who are united with the risen Christ share in this freedom from death's dominion.
6.10: ὃ γὰρ ἀπέθανεν, τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ἀπέθανεν ἐφάπαξ (ho gar apethanen, tē hamartia apethanen ephapax) — "for the death he died, he died to sin once for all." Ephapax (once for all, once for all time) — a term Paul uses only here and in Hebrews-related contexts; the finality and completeness of Christ's single death excludes repetition. ὃ δὲ ζῇ, ζῇ τῷ θεῷ — "but the life he lives, he lives to God" — the present tenses indicate the permanent quality of the resurrection life: it belongs entirely to God.
6.11: λογίζεσθε ἑαυτοὺς νεκροὺς μὲν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ζῶντας δὲ τῷ θεῷ (logizesthe heautous nekrous men tē hamartia zōntas de tō theō) — "reckon yourselves dead to sin but alive to God." Logizesthe (present active imperative: keep reckoning, continue counting) — the same forensic-accounting verb from ch4, now an ethical command. The command is not to make something true by effort but to count as true what is already true in Christ; the reckoning is the believing reception of the accomplished reality.
6.12: μὴ οὖν βασιλευέτω ἡ ἁμαρτία ἐν τῷ θνητῷ ὑμῶν σώματι (mē oun basileuetō hē hamartia en tō thnētō hymōn sōmati) — "let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body." The present imperative with mē can mean: stop what is in progress, or maintain the prevention of what might begin. Thnētō sōmati (mortal body) — sin's domain is the body subject to death; the body is not evil but is the site where sin seeks to re-establish its reign after it has been deposed in principle.
6.13: παραστάνετε ἑαυτοὺς τῷ θεῷ (parastanete heautous tō theō) — "present yourselves to God" — paristēmi (to place beside, present) has a cultic resonance: the priestly presentation of the offering. The believer presents themselves as living offerings (cf. 12:1). ὡσεὶ ἐκ νεκρῶν ζῶντας — "as those who are alive from the dead" — the ethical imperative is grounded in the indicative status: present yourselves as what you actually are — the risen-from-the-dead.
6.14: ἁμαρτία γὰρ ὑμῶν οὐ κυριεύσει (hamartia gar hymōn ou kyrieusei) — "for sin will not be lord over you" — future active: a categorical promise, not merely an aspiration. οὐ γάρ ἐστε ὑπὸ νόμον ἀλλ᾽ ὑπὸ χάριν — "for you are not under law but under grace" — the preposition hypo (under) indicates dominion: "under law" = subject to law's jurisdiction and condemnation; "under grace" = in grace's domain where sin has been dethroned.
6.15: τί οὖν; ἁμαρτήσωμεν ὅτι οὐκ ἐσμὲν ὑπὸ νόμον ἀλλ᾽ ὑπὸ χάριν — the second objection to Paul's grace-teaching; structurally parallel to 6:1 but from a different angle. v1 raised the possibility of continuing in sin under grace; v15 raises the possibility of sinning once because under grace rather than law. The mē genoito dismisses both with equal finality.
6.16: ᾧ παριστάνετε ἑαυτοὺς δούλους εἰς ὑπακοήν — "to whom you present yourselves as slaves for obedience" — the slave-obedience framework: one always serves someone; freedom from sin is not freedom from all masters but transfer to a new master. ἤτοι ἁμαρτίας εἰς θάνατον ἢ ὑπακοῆς εἰς δικαιοσύνην — "either of sin leading to death or of obedience leading to righteousness" — the binary is unavoidable: one's obedience determines one's destiny-direction.
6.17: ὑπηκούσατε δὲ ἐκ καρδίας εἰς ὃν παρεδόθητε τύπον διδαχῆς (hypēkousate de ek kardias eis hon paredothēte typon didachēs) — "you obeyed from the heart the pattern of teaching to which you were handed over." Note the passive: the believers were delivered to the teaching, not just taught it; the teaching takes possession of its recipients. Ek kardias (from the heart) — the obedience is interior and voluntary, not merely behavioral compliance.
6.18: ἐλευθερωθέντες δὲ ἀπὸ τῆς ἁμαρτίας ἐδουλώθητε τῇ δικαιοσύνῃ — "having been freed from sin, you became enslaved to righteousness." Both ēleutherōthentes (freed) and edoulōthēte (enslaved) are aorist passives: both are divine acts, both completed. Freedom from sin is not independence but transfer of ownership — enslaved to righteousness as the new master.
6.19: ἀνθρώπινον λέγω διὰ τὴν ἀσθένειαν τῆς σαρκὸς ὑμῶν — "I am speaking in human terms because of the weakness of your flesh" — Paul acknowledges the inadequacy of the slave-metaphor while using it for pedagogical accessibility. The metaphor breaks down because Christian life under grace is both freedom and service simultaneously; the slave-image captures only the service dimension.
6.20: ἐλεύθεροι ἦτε τῇ δικαιοσύνῃ (eleutheroi ēte tē dikaiosynē) — "you were free from righteousness" — the dative of reference: free with respect to righteousness, i.e., righteousness had no claim on you. The irony is sharp: slavery to sin was experienced as freedom; the "freedom" was in fact maximum bondage.
6.21: τίνα οὖν καρπὸν εἴχετε τότε — "what fruit did you have then?" — the fruit metaphor measures the product of the old life: the karpos (fruit) of the sin-life is shame and death. The harvest of sin reveals its character; Paul invites the Romans to evaluate their pre-conversion life by its actual product.
6.22: εἰς ἁγιασμόν (eis hagiasmon) — "resulting in sanctification" — hagiasmos (holiness, sanctification, the process of becoming holy) — different from hagiasmos as an instantaneous act; the word indicates the ongoing process of being consecrated to God. τέλος δὲ ζωὴν αἰώνιον — "and the end/goal is eternal life" — telos (end, completion, goal) — the harvest-metaphor reaches its eschatological conclusion: eternal life is both the end-of-the-process and the final harvest.
6.23: τὰ γὰρ ὀψώνια τῆς ἁμαρτίας θάνατος (ta gar opsōnia tēs hamartias thanatos) — "for the wages of sin is death." Opsōnion (wage, ration, soldier's pay) — a military term for the wages due a soldier; sin pays its own workers exactly what it owes them. τὸ δὲ χάρισμα τοῦ θεοῦ ζωὴ αἰώνιος ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ τῷ κυρίῳ ἡμῶν — "but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord" — charisma (grace-gift) deliberately contrasted with opsōnion (earned wages): life is not a wage but a gift, not earned but given.

7.1: κυριεύει τοῦ ἀνθρώπου (kyrieuei tou anthrōpou) — "lords it over a person" — kyrieuō (exercise lordship over) with the genitive: the law's dominion is the legal claim it has over the living. The principle Paul will illustrate with the marriage analogy: death terminates legal obligation.
7.2: τῷ ζῶντι ἀνδρὶ δέδεται νόμῳ (tō zōnti andri dedetai nomō) — "she is bound by law to the living husband" — dedetai (perfect passive: has been bound and remains bound) — marriage as a permanent legal obligation while both parties live. ἐὰν δὲ ἀποθάνῃ ὁ ἀνήρ — "but if the husband dies" — death is the one event that dissolves the legal bond; Paul's analogy requires that the believer die (with Christ) to be freed from the law's claim.
7.3: ἐλευθέρα ἐστὶν ἀπὸ τοῦ νόμου (eleuthera estin apo tou nomou) — "she is free from the law" — the marriage analogy establishes the principle: death releases from legal obligation. The wife who remarries while her husband lives is a moichalis (adulteress); but if he has died, the same action is lawful. Paul will apply this: the believer died with Christ and is therefore free to "marry" (be united with) the risen Christ without violating the law.
7.4: ὥστε, ἀδελφοί μου, καὶ ὑμεῖς ἐθανατώθητε τῷ νόμῳ διὰ τοῦ σώματος τοῦ Χριστοῦ — "so then, my brothers and sisters, you also were put to death with regard to the law through the body of Christ." Note the asymmetry with the analogy: in the analogy the husband dies; in the application the wife (believer) dies. Paul's application inverts the subject of death because believers are united with Christ who died; they die through his body, not he through theirs. εἰς τὸ γενέσθαι ὑμᾶς ἑτέρῳ — "so that you might belong to another" — the purpose clause: death-with-Christ enables new belonging to the risen Christ; the new husband is the resurrection life.
7.5: ἐν τῇ σαρκί (en tē sarki) — "in the flesh" — not the physical body per se but the mode of existence characterized by the Adamic nature. The flesh-era is the pre-Christ condition; the Spirit-era is the new-creation condition. τὰ παθήματα τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν τὰ διὰ τοῦ νόμου — "the passions of sins through the law" — the law's role in arousing the very sins it prohibits is Paul's theme; the dia tou nomou (through the law) is the disturbing claim that the holy law is the mechanism through which sinful passions operate.
7.6: κατηργήθημεν ἀπὸ τοῦ νόμου (katērgēthēmen apo tou nomou) — "we have been released from the law" — katargeō again: the law has been rendered inoperative in its claim on those who have died with Christ. ἐν καινότητι πνεύματος καὶ οὐ παλαιότητι γράμματος (en kainotēti pneumatos kai ou palaiōtēti grammatos) — "in newness of Spirit and not in oldness of letter." The pneuma/gramma antithesis (Spirit/letter) is the Pauline expression for the new-covenant/old-covenant distinction; the letter kills (because it condemns without empowering), the Spirit gives life (2 Cor 3:6).
7.7: τί οὖν ἐροῦμεν; ὁ νόμος ἁμαρτία (ti oun eroumen; ho nomos hamartia) — "what then shall we say? Is the law sin?" The implication of v5-6 (sin works through the law): is the law itself sinful? Paul's mē genoito is emphatic. τὴν ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ ἔγνων εἰ μὴ διὰ νόμου — "I would not have known sin except through the law" — the law's diagnostic function: it reveals what sin is by naming it. τὴν ἐπιθυμίαν οὐκ ᾔδειν — "I would not have known covetousness" — the last commandment of the Decalogue (οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις) is the example Paul chooses — perhaps because it addresses the interior desire rather than the external act, making it the commandment that most clearly exposes the depth of sin.
7.8: ἀφορμὴν δὲ λαβοῦσα ἡ ἁμαρτία (aphormēn de labousa hē hamartia) — "but sin, seizing an opportunity" — aphormē (base of operations, starting point for a military expedition); sin uses the commandment as its beachhead. κατειργάσατο ἐν ἐμοὶ πᾶσαν ἐπιθυμίαν — "produced in me all kinds of covetousness" — the commandment's naming of the forbidden desire becomes the stimulus that sin exploits to produce more of the very thing forbidden.
7.9: ἐγὼ δὲ ἔζων χωρὶς νόμου ποτέ (egō de ezōn chōris nomou pote) — "I was once alive apart from the law" — the autobiographical/representative egō describes a pre-commandment state of living. This may be: (a) Paul's own pre-Torah-consciousness childhood; (b) Adam before the commandment; (c) generic Israel before Sinai. All three levels are probably operative. ἐλθούσης δὲ τῆς ἐντολῆς ἡ ἁμαρτία ἀνέζησεν — "but when the commandment came, sin came to life" — aorist: sin's revival is an event triggered by the commandment's arrival.
7.10: καὶ εὑρέθη μοι ἡ ἐντολὴ ἡ εἰς ζωήν, αὕτη εἰς θάνατον — "and the commandment that was intended for life proved to be death for me." The divine intention of the law (eis zōēn = for life, referencing Lev 18:5) and its actual effect in the sin-condition (eis thanaton = for death) are both real; the law is not at fault but sin exploits it. The irony: what was given to produce life produces death because sin perverts it.
7.11: ἡ γὰρ ἁμαρτία ἀφορμὴν λαβοῦσα διὰ τῆς ἐντολῆς ἐξηπάτησέν με (hē gar hamartia aphormēn labousa dia tēs entolēs exēpatēsen me) — "for sin, having seized an opportunity through the commandment, deceived me." Exapataō (to thoroughly deceive) — the Eve-parallel is explicit: as the serpent used the commandment (Gen 3:1-5) to deceive Eve, so sin uses the commandment to deceive the egō. The Genesis allusion is embedded in the verb.
7.12: ὥστε ὁ μὲν νόμος ἅγιος καὶ ἡ ἐντολὴ ἁγία καὶ δικαία καὶ ἀγαθή — "so then the law is holy, and the commandment is holy and just and good." Three predicates clarifying the law's character after the argument that sin exploited it: hagios (consecrated, belonging to God's sphere), dikaios (right by God's standard), agathos (genuinely good in its nature). The law's perversion by sin does not impugn the law; the fault is sin's alone.
7.13: ἀλλὰ ἡ ἁμαρτία, ἵνα φανῇ ἁμαρτία (alla hē hamartia, hina phanē hamartia) — "but sin, so that it might appear as sin." The purpose clause is divine: God allowed sin to work through the good commandment precisely so that sin would be fully exposed as sin — its true character revealed by its exploitation of holiness. ἵνα γένηται καθ᾽ ὑπερβολὴν ἁμαρτωλὸς ἡ ἁμαρτία — "so that sin might become utterly sinful through the commandment" — kath' hyperbolēn (beyond measure, to the extreme) — sin becomes maximally sinful when it uses the holy commandment as its instrument.
7.14: ὁ νόμος πνευματικός ἐστιν, ἐγὼ δὲ σάρκινός εἰμι, πεπραμένος ὑπὸ τὴν ἁμαρτίαν (ho nomos pneumatikos estin, egō de sarkinos eimi, pepramenos hypo tēn hamartian) — "the law is spiritual, but I am fleshly, sold under sin." The contrast pneumatikos/sarkinos (spiritual/fleshly) — the law belongs to God's sphere; the egō belongs to the flesh-sphere. Pepramenos (perfect passive participle: sold and remaining sold) — the slave who has been sold and cannot buy himself back. The famous identity of the egō (Christian? pre-Christian? rhetorical?) is unresolved; the present tense "I am" and "I want" in vv15-24 are the strongest evidence that Paul is describing a Christian condition under the ongoing pull of the Adamic nature.
7.15: ὃ γὰρ κατεργάζομαι οὐ γινώσκω (ho gar katergazomai ou ginōskō) — "for I do not understand what I am doing" — not intellectual ignorance but the breakdown between intention and action that defines the divided self. οὐ γὰρ ὃ θέλω τοῦτο πράσσω, ἀλλ᾽ ὃ μισῶ τοῦτο ποιῶ — "for what I want, this I do not practice; but what I hate, this I do" — the two present-tense verbs of action (prassō, poiō) describe habitual patterns; the thelō (want/will) describes the consistent moral intention that nevertheless fails to translate into action.
7.16: εἰ δὲ ὃ οὐ θέλω τοῦτο ποιῶ, σύμφημι τῷ νόμῳ ὅτι καλός — "but if I do what I do not want, I agree with the law that it is good." The egō's will (thelō) aligns with the law's standard even while actions fail to follow. This alignment of willing-with-law is itself evidence of the law's goodness and of the egō's genuine moral aspiration, however frustrated.
7.17: νυνὶ δὲ οὐκέτι ἐγὼ κατεργάζομαι αὐτό (nyni de ouketi egō katergazomai auto) — "but now it is no longer I who am doing it." The nyni de introduces a crucial distinction: the egō (the true, willing, law-agreeing self) and ἡ οἰκοῦσα ἐν ἐμοὶ ἁμαρτία (hē oikousa en emoi hamartia) — "sin dwelling in me" — oikeō (to dwell, inhabit) as a present participle: sin occupies the egō as an invasive resident. The distinction between the egō and the indwelling sin anticipates the body-resurrection solution.
7.18: οἶδα γὰρ ὅτι οὐκ οἰκεῖ ἐν ἐμοί, τοῦτ᾽ ἔστιν ἐν τῇ σαρκί μου, ἀγαθόν — "for I know that good does not dwell in me, that is, in my flesh." The qualification "that is, in my flesh" is crucial: the egō is not wholly bad; it is the sarx (flesh, the unredeemed bodily existence) in which good does not dwell. τὸ γὰρ θέλειν παράκειταί μοι, τὸ δὲ κατεργάζεσθαι τὸ καλὸν οὔ — "for wanting is present with me, but the doing of good is not" — the vocabulary of presence (parakeimai, to lie beside) contrasts the present-wanting with the absent-doing.
7.19: οὐ γὰρ ὃ θέλω ποιῶ ἀγαθόν, ἀλλ᾽ ὃ οὐ θέλω κακὸν τοῦτο πράσσω — "for the good that I want, I do not do; but the evil I do not want, this I practice." The exact repetition of vv15-16 with the nouns agathon (good) and kakon (evil) specified. The rhetorical repetition is Paul's technique of drilling home the severity of the divided self before the resolution of ch8.
7.20: εἰ δὲ ὃ οὐ θέλω ἐγὼ τοῦτο ποιῶ, οὐκέτι ἐγὼ κατεργάζομαι αὐτό — the same logic as v17 restated: if the egō's will is not the source of the unwanted action, the agent is the indwelling sin. The double ouketi egō (no longer I) in vv17 and 20 emphasizes the dissociation; yet this is not an evasion of responsibility but an analysis of the divided anthropology that requires the Spirit's liberation (ch8) for resolution.
7.21: εὑρίσκω ἄρα τὸν νόμον (heuriskō ara ton nomon) — "so I find the law/principle" — nomos here probably means "principle" or "pattern" rather than the Mosaic Torah; the egō discovers a law-like inevitability in its condition: when it wants to do good, evil is present alongside. The discovery is experiential, not theoretical.
7.22: συνήδομαι γὰρ τῷ νόμῳ τοῦ θεοῦ κατὰ τὸν ἔσω ἄνθρωπον (synēdomai gar tō nomō tou theou kata ton esō anthrōpon) — "for I delight in the law of God according to the inner person." Synēdomai (to rejoice together with, delight in) — a rare verb; the egō's delight in the law of God is genuine and deep. Esō anthrōpos (inner person) — the real self that the Spirit is renewing (2 Cor 4:16; Eph 3:16), in contrast to the flesh that resists the law.
7.23: βλέπω δὲ ἕτερον νόμον ἐν τοῖς μέλεσίν μου ἀντιστρατευόμενον τῷ νόμῳ τοῦ νοός μου — "but I see another law in my members warring against the law of my mind." Antistrateuomai (to wage war against, counter-campaign) — the military metaphor for the conflict within the self: two laws, two armies, the same person as the battleground. αἰχμαλωτίζοντά με τῷ νόμῳ τῆς ἁμαρτίας — "taking me captive to the law of sin" — aichmalōtizō (to take as a prisoner of war); the divided self is the prisoner of the flesh-law even while the mind agrees with God's law.
7.24: ταλαίπωρος ἐγὼ ἄνθρωπος· τίς με ῥύσεται ἐκ τοῦ σώματος τοῦ θανάτου τούτου (talaipōros egō anthrōpos; tis me rhysetai ek tou sōmatos tou thanatou toutou) — "Wretched man that I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death?" Talaipōros (wretched, exhausted from toil and conflict) — the most visceral emotional utterance in the letter. Tis me rhysetai (who will rescue me?) — the future tense: the rescue is still expected, not abandoned. Sōma tou thanatou (body of death) — the mortal body that is the site of sin's indwelling; rescue from this body = the bodily resurrection.
7.25: χάρις δὲ τῷ θεῷ διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν (charis de tō theō dia Iēsou Christou tou kyriou hēmōn) — "thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!" The sudden doxological answer: the rescue of v24 is already known; the thanksgiving anticipates the full exposition of ch8. Ἄρα οὖν αὐτὸς ἐγὼ τῷ μὲν νοῒ δουλεύω νόμῳ θεοῦ τῇ δὲ σαρκὶ νόμῳ ἁμαρτίας — "so then, I myself serve God's law with my mind but with my flesh I serve the law of sin." The summary restates the divided condition; the ch7 egō remains divided until the Spirit's liberation in ch8. Paul does not resolve the tension in ch7 but in ch8 — the dramatic movement of the letter.

15.1: ὀφείλομεν (we are obligated, G3784) — the verb is of debt and moral necessity, not mere preference. Paul includes himself in the "we who are strong," establishing that the obligation to bear with the weak is a defining characteristic of the strong, not a concession they may choose to make. βαστάζειν (to carry, bear, G941) — the infinitive of purpose; the same verb used for carrying burdens in Gal 6:2, where it describes mutual burden-bearing in the community.
15.2: ἕκαστος ἡμῶν τῷ πλησίον ἀρεσκέτω (let each of us please his neighbor) — present imperative: an ongoing obligation. εἰς οἰκοδομήν (for building up, G3619) — the construction metaphor that runs through Paul's ethics; pleasing the neighbor is not mere social compliance but serves the architectural goal of the community's flourishing. The phrase eis to agathon (for the good) qualifies areskō — pleasing must be directed toward actual good, not flattery.
15.3: καὶ γὰρ ὁ Χριστὸς οὐχ ἑαυτῷ ἤρεσεν (for Christ himself did not please himself) — aorist indicative: a completed historical act. The negative is emphatic; Christ's whole Incarnation and Passion is described as a sustained refusal of self-pleasing. τοῦ μὴ ἀρέσαι ἑαυτῷ — articular infinitive used as substantive subject of the quotation's application: the result of Christ's not-pleasing-himself is the weight of others' insults falling on him (Ps 69:9 LXX).
15.4: ὅσα γὰρ προεγράφη (whatever was written beforehand, G4270) — aorist passive of prographō; the divine passive indicates God as the author of Scripture's purpose. εἰς τὴν ἡμετέραν διδασκαλίαν ἐγράφη (was written for our instruction) — another aorist passive; the purpose is explicitly pedagogical for the present community. διὰ τῆς ὑπομονῆς καὶ διὰ τῆς παρακλήσεως τῶν γραφῶν — through the patient endurance and the encouragement of the Scriptures; the genitives are subjective: Scripture itself produces these qualities in the reader.
15.5: ὁ θεὸς τῆς ὑπομονῆς καὶ τῆς παρακλήσεως (the God of endurance and encouragement) — Paul's characteristic way of naming God by the gifts he gives; the same divine attributes that the Scriptures produce (v.4) are sourced in God's own character. τὸ αὐτὸ φρονεῖν ἐν ἀλλήλοις κατὰ Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν (to think the same thing toward one another according to Christ Jesus) — phroneo (to think/be minded, G5426) is the attitude/disposition verb Paul uses throughout Phil 2; the unity requested is not intellectual uniformity but the same orientation toward the other.
15.6: ὁμοθυμαδόν (with one accord, G3661) — a compound adverb built from homos (same) + thymos (passionate impulse, spirit); it appears frequently in Acts for the gathered community. The word describes a unified orientation of the will, not merely coordinated action. ἐν ἑνὶ στόματι (with one mouth) — the communal worship that results from the unified disposition; the glorification is verbal and liturgical, not merely attitudinal.
15.7: προσλαμβάνεσθε ἀλλήλους (receive/accept one another, G4355) — present middle imperative: the receiving is to be an active, ongoing practice. The same verb appears at 14:1 and 14:3 where God's receiving of both weak and strong is the ground for mutual acceptance. καθὼς καὶ ὁ Χριστὸς προσελάβετο ὑμᾶς (just as Christ received you) — aorist: a single completed act that defines the character of the community's ongoing receiving of each other.
15.8:
διάκονον γεγενῆσθαι περιτομῆς (to have become a servant/deacon of the circumcised, G1249) — the perfect infinitive indicates an ongoing resultant state; Christ's Incarnation placed him in the continuing role of servant to Israel. Diakonos here is the same word used for Phoebe in 16:1 — a functionary serving a community's need. ὑπὲρ ἀληθείας θεοῦ (on behalf of God's truth) — genitive of the beneficiary; the mission to Israel serves the integrity of God's own covenant word.

εἰς τὸ βεβαιῶσαι τὰς ἐπαγγελίας τῶν πατέρων (to confirm the promises to the patriarchs, G950) — articular infinitive of purpose; bebaiōsai (to make firm, establish) is the verb for confirming a guarantee. Christ's mission to Israel was not a postponed mission to Gentiles but the necessary fulfillment of covenant promises that then opened the way for the Gentiles.
15.9: ὑπὲρ ἐλέους (on behalf of/for mercy, G1656) — here the Gentile glorification is grounded in God's mercy, contrasted with Israel's covenant-promise ground (v.8); both are reasons for the single mission. ἐξομολογήσομαί σοι ἐν ἔθνεσιν (I will praise you among the Gentiles) — future middle of exomologeō; the quotation from Ps 18:49/2 Sam 22:50 places the Davidic king among the nations in praise, which Paul reads as a messianic figure whose praise extends Israel's worship into Gentile territory.
15.10: The quotation from Deut 32:43 (the Song of Moses) — εὐφράνθητε, ἔθνη, μετὰ τοῦ λαοῦ αὐτοῦ (rejoice, Gentiles, with his people) — is a call for Gentile participation in Israel's covenantal joy. Moses's own closing poem already envisioned Gentiles rejoicing alongside Israel; Paul reads this as anticipation of the Gentile mission (cf. 10:19 where he quotes Deut 32:21 from the same poem).
15.11: Ps 117:1 (LXX 116:1) — αἰνεῖτε τὸν κύριον πάντα τὰ ἔθνη (praise the Lord all the Gentiles) — the shortest psalm and the broadest invitation; Paul uses it as a third OT witness to Gentile worship. The fourfold OT catena (vv.9-12) from Psalms, Deuteronomy, Psalms, Isaiah demonstrates that the Gentile mission is not an afterthought but the convergence of multiple scriptural promises.
15.12: ἡ ῥίζα τοῦ Ἰεσσαί (the root of Jesse) — Paul quotes Isa 11:10 LXX; the Hebrew of Isa 11:10 has "root of Jesse" as a banner/signal, but the LXX (and Paul) read it as the one who rises up to rule. ὁ ἀνιστάμενος ἄρχειν ἐθνῶν (the one who rises to rule over the nations) — anistamenos is a present middle participle that can carry the double sense of rising geographically and rising from death; Paul may intend both the Messiah's reign and his resurrection as the ground of Gentile hope.
15.13: ὁ θεὸς τῆς ἐλπίδος (the God of hope, G1680) — the title summarizes the OT catena just completed: the God whose promises to patriarchs, Psalms, Deuteronomy, and Isaiah all pointed toward the hope now declared. πληρώσαι ὑμᾶς πάσης χαρᾶς καὶ εἰρήνης ἐν τῷ πιστεύειν (may he fill you with all joy and peace in believing) — aorist optative: the wish-prayer form; en tō pisteuein locates the filling within the ongoing act of trust. εἰς τὸ περισσεύειν ὑμᾶς ἐν τῇ ἐλπίδι (so that you overflow in hope) — perisseuein (G4052) is not merely having hope but brimming over with it; the goal of filling is abundance beyond sufficiency. ἐν δυνάμει πνεύματος ἁγίου (by power of the Holy Spirit) — the Spirit is the agent of the overflowing.
15.14: πεπληρωμένοι πάσης γνώσεως (filled with all knowledge, G1108) — the perfect passive participle indicates a standing state, not a process being initiated; Paul is not starting the Roman community's education but confirming what is already present. νουθετεῖν ἀλλήλους (to admonish/counsel one another, G3560) — noutheteō is a strong verb for corrective guidance; it implies both knowledge of the standard and will to apply it in the community context.
15.15: τολμηρότερον (quite boldly, comparative adverb, G5112) — the comparative form is an acknowledgment that some parts of the letter have pushed beyond courteous encouragement into frank challenge. ὡς ἐπαναμιμνῄσκων ὑμᾶς (as reminding you) — the participle frames Paul's letter as re-activating knowledge already possessed, not imposing foreign doctrine. διὰ τὴν χάριν τὴν δοθεῖσάν μοι (because of the grace given to me) — the aorist passive participle marks the apostolic commission as a gift received, not an authority self-claimed; cf. the same formula at Rom 12:3 and 1 Cor 3:10.
15.16:
λειτουργὸν Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ (liturgical servant/minister of Christ Jesus, G3011) — leitourgos is a civic and religious term for one who performs public service at their own expense; it carries cultic/priestly resonance. Paul frames his apostolic role in priestly vocabulary to ground what follows. ἱερουργοῦντα τὸ εὐαγγέλιον (performing priestly service with/for the gospel, G2418) — hierourgeō is a hapax in the NT; it means to handle something as a priest handles the sacred. The gospel is the sacred object of Paul's priestly service.

ἡ προσφορὰ τῶν ἐθνῶν (the offering of the Gentiles, G4376) — an ambiguous genitive: the Gentiles as the offering (objective), or the offering that the Gentiles bring (subjective). The context suggests the objective reading: Paul presents the converted Gentiles to God as a priestly offering. εὐπρόσδεκτος ἡγιασμένη ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ (acceptable, sanctified by the Holy Spirit) — the Spirit performs the sanctification that makes the human offering acceptable, taking over the role the altar ritual performed for animal offerings.
15.17: τὴν καύχησιν ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ (the boasting in Christ Jesus) — Paul's boasting is qualified by its location: not in his own achievements but in Christ. The dative en Christō specifies the sphere; his basis for glory is Christ's work, not his own. τὰ πρὸς τὸν θεόν (the things toward God) — the specific domain of his priestly role (cf. Heb 2:17; 5:1).
15.18: οὐ γὰρ τολμήσω τι λαλεῖν ὧν οὐ κατειργάσατο Χριστὸς δι᾽ ἐμοῦ (I will not dare to speak of anything except what Christ accomplished through me) — the double negative creates emphatic restriction; Paul's preaching authority is entirely derivative. κατειργάσατο (aorist middle = accomplished, G2716) — Christ is the subject; Paul is the instrument. εἰς ὑπακοὴν ἐθνῶν (for the obedience of the Gentiles) — the same eis hypakoēn frame as 1:5; the missionary goal is framed as covenant allegiance (obedience of faith).
15.19: ἐν δυνάμει σημείων καὶ τεράτων (in the power of signs and wonders, G4592/G5059) — the pairing sēmeia kai terata is the standard OT phrase for miraculous acts accompanying divine missions (Exod 7:3; Deut 6:22); Paul claims this category for his apostolic mission. κεκληρωκέναι (perfect active infinitive of G2820, to complete/fulfill) — the perfect tense is significant: Paul's encirclement of the eastern Mediterranean with the gospel is presented as a completed mission in those regions, not an ongoing one. ἀπὸ Ἰερουσαλὴμ... μέχρι τοῦ Ἰλλυρικοῦ (from Jerusalem to Illyricum) — the geographical arc traces the actual mission; Illyricum is the northwest Balkan region, the farthest point Paul had reached.
15.20: φιλοτιμούμενον (G5389, making it my ambition) — a significant word; philotimeomai means to seek honor in a particular direction, to aspire deliberately; it is an honorable ambition rather than mere preference. εὐαγγελίζεσθαι οὐχ ὅπου ὠνομάσθη Χριστός (to proclaim the gospel where Christ has not been named) — aorist passive ōnomasthē (was named); the criterion for Paul's geographical planning is not institutional but pioneering: unplanted rather than unwatered. ἐπ᾽ ἀλλότριον θεμέλιον οἰκοδομῶ (I am building on another's foundation) — the construction metaphor Paul uses at 1 Cor 3:10 for the relationship between apostles and subsequent teachers.
15.21: Paul quotes Isa 52:15 LXX — ὄψονται, οἷς οὐκ ἀνηγγέλη (those who were not told will see) — as the scriptural warrant for his pioneer-mission strategy. Isaiah's Servant Song announces that kings will be silenced and see what they had not been told; Paul reads the Servant's mission as defining the scope of his own: going where the message has not yet gone. The scriptural grounding of his missional method is consistent with the letter's argument that the gospel's reach is scripturally anticipated.
15.22: τὰ πολλὰ ἐνεκοπτόμην (I was often/repeatedly hindered, G1465) — the imperfect passive indicates repeated action in the past; the hindering was not a single obstacle but a pattern. Paul does not specify the hindrance; it may be providential direction (cf. Acts 16:6-7), competing ministry demands, or adversarial opposition.
15.23: μηκέτι τόπον ἔχων ἐν τοῖς κλίμασιν τούτοις (no longer having place in these regions, G5117) — the "place" is the available space for pioneer proclamation; once the gospel is planted, Paul's work in that region is complete. ἐπιποθίαν δὲ ἔχων τοῦ ἐλθεῖν πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἀπὸ πολλῶν ἐτῶν (having a deep longing to come to you for many years, G1972) — epipothia is an intensified form of longing, beyond ordinary desire; the duration (many years) underlines the genuineness.
15.24: θεάσωμαι ὑμᾶς (behold/see you, G2300) — theaomai has a richer visual dimension than the plain horaō; it suggests a purposeful beholding rather than a passing sight. ὑμῶν προπεμφθῆναι ἐκεῖ (to be sent forward/assisted by you, G4311) — propempō is a technical term for the practical support (provisions, letters, travel companions) that a community provided for an itinerant missionary at departure.
15.25: διακονῶν τοῖς ἁγίοις (serving the saints, G1247) — present participle indicating the ongoing current activity that delays the Roman visit. The same diakoneo root appears throughout ch.15 (v.8 Christ as diakonos, v.16 Paul as leitourgos, 16:1 Phoebe as diakonos); the term has both formal and informal service dimensions.
15.26: κοινωνίαν τινὰ ποιήσασθαι (to make a contribution/fellowship, G2842) — koinōnia carries both the spiritual dimension (participation, fellowship) and the practical (the financial sharing that expresses it). The same word Paul uses for the Lord's Supper in 1 Cor 10:16. εἰς τοὺς πτωχοὺς τῶν ἁγίων (for the poor among the saints) — the poverty is specific to Jerusalem; the collection is an act of Gentile solidarity with the mother community.
15.27: ὀφείλουσιν (they owe it, G3784) — the same obligation-verb as 15:1 (we are obligated); obligation here is not legal but covenantal and relational. τοῖς πνευματικοῖς αὐτῶν ἐκοινώνησαν (they shared in their spiritual things, G2841) — the reciprocity Paul identifies is ontological and economic: Gentiles received spiritual inheritance through Jewish covenant; Jews now receive material support from Gentiles. The sharing (koinōnōnō) is the same root as koinōnia in v.26.
15.28: σφραγισάμενος αὐτοῖς τὸν καρπὸν τοῦτον (having sealed this fruit to them, G4972) — sphragizō is the verb for sealing as certification of delivery; Paul is treating the collection as a sealed consignment to be formally handed over. καρπόν (fruit, G2590) — the mission-harvest term (cf. 1:13; 15:20); the Jerusalem collection is itself described as fruit, implying it is the legitimate yield of the Gentile mission.
15.29: ἐν πληρώματι εὐλογίας Χριστοῦ (in the fullness of the blessing of Christ) — plēroma (G4138) is Paul's fullness-word; the blessing he will bring is not partial or provisional but complete. This confidence is grounded in Christ's provision, not Paul's preparation.
15.30: συναγωνίσασθαί μοι (to strive together with me, G4865) — the compound verb is from the arena; prayer is framed as athletic contest requiring real effort alongside the one praying. ἐν ταῖς προσευχαῖς ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ πρὸς τὸν θεόν (in prayers on my behalf to God) — the double prepositions specify both direction (toward God) and beneficiary (on my behalf). διὰ τῆς ἀγάπης τοῦ πνεύματος (by/through the love of the Spirit, G4151 disp=3) — the genitive is probably subjective: the love that the Spirit generates in the community becomes the motivating ground of their prayer-striving.
15.31: ῥυσθῶ ἀπὸ τῶν ἀπειθούντων ἐν τῇ Ἰουδαίᾳ (that I may be delivered from the unbelieving/disobedient ones in Judea, G544) — apeithountes = those who disobey, refuse to be persuaded; the same root as 11:30-32 where it describes Gentile and Jewish disobedience. Paul's prayer request anticipates the actual opposition he will face in Jerusalem (Acts 21:27-36). ἡ διακονία (the ministry/service, G1248) — the collection for Jerusalem is elevated to the status of a formal ministry.
15.32: ἵνα ἐν χαρᾷ ἐλθὼν πρὸς ὑμᾶς διὰ θελήματος θεοῦ (that coming to you with joy by God's will) — dia thelēmatos theou qualifies the arrival as conditional on divine permission; the joy is the anticipated texture of a visit completed under favorable conditions. συναναπαύσωμαι ὑμῖν (I may be refreshed/rested together with you, G4875) — the compound verb implies mutual refreshment; Paul anticipates the visit as mutually restorative, not merely ministerially productive (cf. 1:12).
15.33: ὁ θεὸς τῆς εἰρήνης (the God of peace, G1515) — the last in the series of divine-character titles in this chapter (God of endurance v.5, God of hope v.13); this one is both a closing blessing and a forward pointer to 16:20 where the same title appears with eschatological content ("will soon crush Satan"). The peace Paul invokes is not merely relational but the comprehensive OT shalom — wholeness and right ordering.

16.1: Φοίβην τὴν ἀδελφὴν ἡμῶν (Phoebe our sister) — the kinship designation adelphē marks full membership in the covenant community, not a subordinate category. οὖσαν καὶ διάκονον τῆς ἐκκλησίας (who is also a deacon/servant of the church, G1249) — diakonos has no feminine form in Greek; it applies without modification to Phoebe. The word is used for Christ in 15:8, for Paul in 1 Cor 3:5, and for community officers in Phil 1:1 and 1 Tim 3:8. The same term for the same role, regardless of the referent's gender.
16.2: ἀξίως τῶν ἁγίων (in a manner worthy of the saints) — the genitive of standard: the quality of reception is calibrated to what membership in the holy people deserves. προστάτις πολλῶν ἐγενήθη καὶ ἐμοῦ αὐτοῦ (she has been a patron/helper of many and of me myself, G4368) — prostatis (G4368) is the feminine of prostates, a term from civic life for a patron or sponsor who provides legal support and financial backing for persons lacking status. Paul explicitly names himself as among her beneficiaries, confirming the substantive nature of the role.
16.3: Πρίσκαν καὶ Ἀκύλαν (Priscilla and Aquila) — Priscilla is named first; across the six NT references to this couple, she is named first in four. Ancient convention generally placed higher-status individuals first; the consistent pattern likely reflects her leadership role. τοὺς συνεργούς μου ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ (my co-workers in Christ Jesus, G4904) — synergos is Paul's term for collaborative ministry partners (cf. Phil 4:3; 1 Thess 3:2); applied equally here to Priscilla and Aquila together.
16.4: τὸν ἑαυτῶν τράχηλον ὑπέθηκαν (they laid down their own necks, G5137) — an idiom for risking execution; the image is of baring one's throat to a blade. The reflexive heautōn emphasizes the personal cost. πᾶσαι αἱ ἐκκλησίαι τῶν ἐθνῶν (all the churches of the Gentiles) — a broad acknowledgment indicating that Priscilla and Aquila's sacrifice was known and appreciated across the Pauline network.
16.5: τὴν κατ᾽ οἶκον αὐτῶν ἐκκλησίαν (the church in their house, G1577) — the house-church is the primary organizational unit of early Christianity; there is no dedicated church building in this period. ἀπαρχὴν τῆς Ἀσίας (first fruit of Asia, G536) — the same term Paul uses for the Spirit (8:23) and for the resurrection (1 Cor 15:20); applying it to an individual describes Epaenetus as the initial yield of the gospel harvest in the province, consecrating the whole to God.
16.6: ἥτις πολλὰ ἐκοπίασεν εἰς ὑμᾶς (who labored much for you, G2872) — kopiaō is Paul's word for intensive labor (used for agricultural and manual work); it appears frequently in his accounts of apostolic ministry (1 Cor 15:10; Gal 4:11; Phil 2:16). The application of this term to Mary affirms the substantive and effortful nature of her contribution to the Roman community.
16.7:
Ἀνδρόνικον καὶ Ἰουνίαν (Andronicus and Junia) — the second name is almost certainly Iounian (accusative of Iounia, a common Roman female name) rather than Iounian (accusative of the unattested male name Iounias); the female reading is supported by the standard Greek accent and by the absence of the name Junias in any ancient source. ἐπίσημοι ἐν τοῖς ἀποστόλοις (outstanding among the apostles) — the phrase is debated: either (a) notable/distinguished within the group of apostles, or (b) well-known in the eyes of the apostles. Option (a) would make Junia an apostle; option (b) would not. The lexical parallel with other en-constructions favors the inclusive reading.

οἳ καὶ πρὸ ἐμοῦ γέγοναν ἐν Χριστῷ (who also were in Christ before me) — the perfect tense gegonasin indicates a state persisting from before Paul's conversion; they are senior to him in the faith.
16.8: τὸν ἀγαπητόν μου ἐν κυρίῳ (my beloved in the Lord) — the en kyriō qualifier is consistent throughout the greeting list; it specifies that the love and relationship is grounded in their shared belonging to Christ, not merely social affinity.
16.9: Οὐρβανὸν τὸν συνεργὸν ἡμῶν ἐν Χριστῷ (Urbanus our co-worker in Christ) — the synergos title again (cf. v.3); its consistent use marks those who have participated directly in the gospel mission alongside Paul.
16.10: τὸν δόκιμον ἐν Χριστῷ (the approved/tested one in Christ, G1384) — dokimos (the positive of adokimos, the disqualified mind of 1:28) means one who has stood the test, proven genuine. τοὺς ἐκ τῶν Ἀριστοβούλου (those from the household of Aristobulus) — not Aristobulus himself (who may not have been a believer) but members of his household.
16.11: τὸν συγγενῆ μου (my kinsman/fellow Jew, G4773) — syngenēs can mean blood relative or ethnic kin (fellow Jew); given the usage pattern in this section, ethnic kinship (fellow Jew) is more likely.
16.12: Τρύφαιναν καὶ Τρυφῶσαν τὰς κοπιώσας ἐν κυρίῳ (Tryphena and Tryphosa, those who work hard in the Lord, G2872) — kopiaousas (present participle) again: both women are characterized by ongoing labor in the community. The names mean "dainty/soft" (Tryphena) and "delicate" (Tryphosa) — possibly sisters, given the similar names. Περσίδα τὴν ἀγαπητήν, ἥτις πολλὰ ἐκοπίασεν ἐν κυρίῳ (beloved Persis, who labored much in the Lord) — the aorist ekopiasein for Persis vs. present participle for Tryphena/Tryphosa may indicate that Persis's intensive work was in the past, while the others' continues.
16.13: Ῥοῦφον τὸν ἐκλεκτὸν ἐν κυρίῳ (Rufus, the chosen one in the Lord, G1588) — eklektos (elect/chosen) is the standard term for divine election; whether it is a common designation here for any believer or a specific honorific is unclear. τὴν μητέρα αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐμοῦ (his mother and mine) — the biological mother of Rufus who has functioned as a mother to Paul; a profound statement of the re-family that the new covenant creates.
16.14: The four names in v.14 are followed by τοὺς σὺν αὐτοῖς ἀδελφούς (the brothers/siblings with them) — indicating a house-church or small group attached to these individuals. The pattern repeats in v.15. Multiple overlapping house-churches in Rome (vv.5, 10, 11, 14, 15) reflects the decentralized social organization of the Roman church.
16.15: The greeting to Philologus, Julia, Nereus, his sister, and Olympas concludes the individual and household greetings. πάντων τῶν ἁγίων τῶν σὺν αὐτοῖς (all the saints with them) — the community Paul is addressing is distributed across multiple households; the letter functions as communication to the whole network.
16.16: φιλήματι ἁγίῳ (with a holy kiss, G5370) — the greeting practice appears in all four major Pauline letters (1 Cor 16:20; 2 Cor 13:12; 1 Thess 5:26); its designation as "holy" distinguishes it from secular social kissing by marking it as a covenant-community act. αἱ ἐκκλησίαι πᾶσαι τοῦ Χριστοῦ (all the churches of Christ) — the final greeting extends the apostolic network's endorsement to the Roman community.
16.17: σκοπεῖν τοὺς τὰς διχοστασίας καὶ τὰ σκάνδαλα ποιοῦντας (watch out for those making divisions and stumbling blocks, G1370/G4625) — skopeō (G4648) is attentive watching, not passive observation; dichostasia (divisions) and skandala (stumbling-blocks, traps) are the double threat. παρὰ τὴν διδαχὴν ἣν ὑμεῖς ἐμάθετε (contrary to the teaching you learned) — the aorist emathete treats the received teaching as the stable norm against which deviations are measured.
16.18: τῇ ἑαυτῶν κοιλίᾳ δουλεύουσιν (they are enslaved to their own belly/appetite, G2836) — koilia (stomach/belly) is used contemptuously for self-indulgent, appetite-driven behavior (cf. Phil 3:19); these teachers serve their own material interests under religious cover. χρηστολογίας καὶ εὐλογίας (smooth talk and flattery, G5543/G2129) — chrēstologia is a rare compound (G5543) meaning plausible, fair-sounding speech; eulogia here in the unusual negative sense of fine-sounding empty praise. The two terms describe the rhetorical technique of the troublemakers.
16.19: ἐφ᾽ ὑμῖν χαίρω (I rejoice over/because of you) — epi + dative marks the ground of joy: the Roman community's obedience itself. θέλω δὲ ὑμᾶς σοφοὺς εἶναι εἰς τὸ ἀγαθόν (but I want you to be wise for good) — the infinitive construction specifies the direction of wisdom: toward good rather than merely smart. ἀκεραίους δὲ εἰς τὸ κακόν (innocent/unmixed regarding evil, G185) — akeraios means unadulterated, unmixed; the image is of undiluted material not contaminated by evil.
16.20: ὁ θεὸς τῆς εἰρήνης συντρίψει τὸν Σατανᾶν ὑπὸ τοὺς πόδας ὑμῶν ἐν τάχει (the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet) — syntribō (G4937) is a strong crushing verb; en tachei (speedily, soon) indicates near-future eschatological expectation. This alludes to Gen 3:15 (the head of the serpent crushed); Paul applies the promise to the community's cosmic struggle with the adversary. The God of peace crushing the adversary is not paradoxical: peace requires the defeat of what opposes it.
