16.21: Τιμόθεος ὁ συνεργός μου (Timothy my co-worker, G4904) — the first of the sender's greetings from Paul's companions; synergos used for Timothy as in v.3 for Priscilla and Aquila. Λούκιος καὶ Ἰάσων καὶ Σωσίπατρος οἱ συγγενεῖς μου (Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater, my kinsmen) — the kinship designation likely means fellow Jews (cf. v.11) rather than blood relatives.
16.22: ἐγὼ Τέρτιος ὁ γράψας τὴν ἐπιστολὴν (I, Tertius, who wrote the letter) — a rare moment when the professional scribe (amanuensis) identifies himself; Paul would have dictated and Tertius wrote. The phrase ἐν κυρίῳ (in the Lord) characterizes Tertius's own greeting, indicating he was a believer, not merely a hired secretary.
16.23: Γάϊος ὁ ξένος μου καὶ ὅλης τῆς ἐκκλησίας (Gaius my host and of the whole church, G3581) — xenos here means host (one who receives guests), not stranger; Gaius's house served as Paul's base and the gathering place for the whole church in Corinth. Ἔραστος ὁ οἰκονόμος τῆς πόλεως (Erastus the city treasurer/steward, G3623) — oikonomos tēs poleōs is the financial administrator of the city; an inscription found at Corinth mentions an Erastus who paved a street at his own expense. If the same person, his conversion is socially significant.
16.24: ἡ χάρις τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ) — the standard Pauline benediction; some manuscripts omit v.24 as textually suspect (it may be a scribal insertion assimilating to the Thessalonian epistles). The doxology that follows (vv.25-27) is the letter's formal close.
16.25: τῷ δὲ δυναμένῳ ὑμᾶς στηρίξαι (now to him who is able to establish you, G4741) — the doxology opens with the divine capacity to do what Paul has been praying for throughout the letter (cf. 1:11). κατὰ τὸ εὐαγγέλιόν μου καὶ τὸ κήρυγμα Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (according to my gospel and the proclamation of Jesus Christ) — the dual genitive: the gospel as Paul proclaims it, and the proclamation whose content is Jesus Christ. κατὰ ἀποκάλυψιν μυστηρίου χρόνοις αἰωνίοις σεσιγημένου (according to the revelation of mystery silent in ages past, G3466) — mystērion in Paul is not an esoteric secret but a divinely hidden plan now disclosed; sesigēmenou (perfect passive participle = having been kept silent) emphasizes the completed divine concealment now ending.
16.26: φανερωθέντος δὲ νῦν (but now having been disclosed, G5319) — the aorist passive marks the disclosure as the eschatological event; nyn is the same temporal marker as 3:21 (nyni = but now). διά τε γραφῶν προφητικῶν (also through prophetic writings) — the mystery is disclosed both through the gospel event and through the OT Scriptures; the letter's entire argument about the Law and Prophets bearing witness (3:21) is here summarized. εἰς ὑπακοὴν πίστεως (for the obedience of faith) — the closing bracket of the letter's framing (cf. 1:5); the same phrase that described Paul's mission purpose in the salutation now describes the goal of the mystery's disclosure to all nations.
16.27: μόνῳ σοφῷ θεῷ (to the only wise God) — the dative of indirect object of the glory-ascription. Monō sophō (only wise) contrasts the transcendent wisdom of God with the pretended wisdom of 1:22 ("claiming to be wise, they became fools"). The letter closes where it began: the gospel as the disclosure of divine wisdom to those who were without excuse before God's wisdom in creation. διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (through Jesus Christ) — the mediatorial role persists even in the doxological address; glory reaches God through and in Christ, not apart from him.

8.1: κατάκριμα (katakrima) is the adverse verdict — the condemning sentence, not merely the feeling of guilt. Its negation here is forensic: the courtroom record has been cleared. The construction οὐδὲν κατάκριμα τοῖς ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ sets "in Christ Jesus" as the jurisdictional sphere where the verdict does not hold; the prepositional phrase does structural work the English "for" can obscure.
8.2: νόμος appears twice with different referents: the "law of the Spirit of life" and the "law of sin and death." The word operates here as "governing principle" rather than Torah; the genitive "of life" marks the Spirit as the source of that life. The perfect ἠλευθέρωσεν (has set free) describes an accomplished liberation whose effects continue to hold.
8.3:
The phrase περὶ ἁμαρτίας (peri hamartias) is the LXX technical term for the sin offering (Lev 5:7; 7:2). Paul is placing the Incarnation inside the sacrificial framework of Torah. The phrase ἐν ὁμοιώματι σαρκὸς ἁμαρτίας (in the likeness of sinful flesh) is careful: ὁμοίωμα (likeness) means Christ shares the form of fallen humanity without being constituted by its sin — real solidarity, hedged identity.

κατέκρινεν τὴν ἁμαρτίαν ἐν τῇ σαρκί — he condemned sin in the flesh: the condemnation happens inside the very sphere sin had occupied.
8.4: δικαίωμα (dikaiōma) — a specific righteous requirement or ordinance, not the general quality of righteousness (dikaiosynē). The law's dikaiōma is now fulfilled ἐν ἡμῖν (in us) — passive, not active: it is accomplished in us, not by us. The present participle περιπατοῦσιν (who walk) identifies ongoing orientation rather than perfect performance.
8.5: φρόνημα (phronēma) — mind-set, inclination, or habitual orientation, not simply "mind" or "thoughts." The verb φρονέω covers the whole set of one's mental dispositions and longings. Those who are κατὰ σάρκα (according to the flesh) are oriented toward what the flesh prizes; the contrast with κατὰ πνεῦμα is totalizing — about which domain governs the person's entire orientation.
8.6: φρόνημα σαρκός is death; φρόνημα πνεύματος is life and peace. The Greek places the predicate nominatives without a linking verb in the second clause (asyndeton), making the identification stark: this orientation is death; that orientation is life and peace. The double outcome "life and peace" is covenantal shorthand for eschatological blessing (cf. Num 25:12; Isa 54:10).
8.7: ἔχθρα εἰς θεόν — enmity toward God, not merely estrangement from God. The flesh's mind-set is positionally hostile; it does not merely fail to obey but is constitutionally opposed (οὐχ ὑποτάσσεται, does not submit). The double οὐ δύναται (cannot) marks impossibility, not merely difficulty.
8.8: οἱ ἐν σαρκί (those in the flesh) does not refer to embodied existence per se — Paul assumes Christians remain embodied — but to those whose identity is located in and governed by the sphere of σάρξ. The inability to please God is structural, not situational.
8.9: εἴπερ (since indeed) — a first-class conditional that treats the condition as factually assumed. Paul does not say "if the Spirit happens to live in you"; the eiper treats it as established. The equation "Spirit of God" = "Spirit of Christ" is stated as a matter of course, presupposing Trinitarian identification of the Spirit's source and person.
8.10: Two parallel διά constructions with accusative (causative): the body is dead διὰ ἁμαρτίαν (because of sin), but the spirit is alive διὰ δικαιοσύνην (because of righteousness). The lower-case "spirit" here most naturally refers to the human spirit — the inner person — being animated by God's righteousness, not to the Holy Spirit (contrast v. 9).
8.11: The argument moves from past (the Spirit raised Christ) to future (the Spirit will also give life to mortal bodies). The prepositional phrase διὰ τοῦ ἐνοικοῦντος αὐτοῦ πνεύματος (through his Spirit who dwells in you) uses the genitive of means: the Spirit is the agent of the bodily resurrection. This is the passage's strongest statement connecting pneumatology to bodily resurrection.
8.12: ὀφειλέται (debtors/those under obligation) — the debt metaphor; Paul uses it negatively here. Having been freed from the flesh's governance creates no debt to the flesh. The verse prepares the logic of vv. 13–14: obligation runs in the other direction entirely.
8.13: θανατοῦτε (put to death) — present active imperative, indicating ongoing action: keep mortifying the practices of the body. The agent of this mortification is πνεύματι (by the Spirit) — Spirit-empowered discipline, not autonomous effort. The antithesis is stark: live according to the flesh and you die; mortify the flesh's practices by the Spirit and you live.
8.14: ἄγονται (are led) — present passive: those who are in an ongoing state of being led by the Spirit of God — these are υἱοὶ θεοῦ (sons of God). The participle ὅσοι (as many as) is inclusive: sonship is defined by Spirit-leadership rather than by ethnic identity.
8.15: υἱοθεσίας (huiothesias) — adoption/placing-as-son; a legal-relational term found only in Paul in the NT (here, 8:23; 9:4; Gal 4:5; Eph 1:5). The contrast with πνεῦμα δουλείας (spirit of slavery) maps onto the Exodus pattern: slavery gave way to covenant sonship. Ἀββᾶ ὁ πατήρ — the Aramaic address Abba followed by its Greek equivalent; the bilingual cry is not liturgical formula but the spontaneous address of intimacy, the same term Jesus uses in Gethsemane (Mark 14:36).
8.16: συμμαρτυρεῖ (co-witnesses, bears witness alongside) — the Spirit's testimony is not separate from our spirit's cry but joint: our spirit cries "Abba" and the Holy Spirit witnesses alongside that cry, confirming its validity. The syn- prefix marks participation, not replacement.
8.17: κληρονόμοι (heirs) + συγκληρονόμοι (co-heirs with Christ) — four syn- compound verbs in vv. 17–29 mark coparticipation: sympaschomen (suffer-together), syndoxasthōmen (glorified-together). The condition εἴπερ συμπάσχομεν uses the same eiper as v. 9 — treating shared suffering as the present form that shared glory will follow.
8.18: λογίζομαι (I reckon/calculate) — a deliberate act of estimation, not merely an opinion; Paul uses the verb elsewhere for accounting transactions (4:3–8). The present sufferings are subjected to rational evaluation and judged not comparable. ἀποκαλυφθῆναι — future passive infinitive; the glory is hidden, awaiting eschatological uncovering.
8.19: ἀποκαραδοκία (apokaradokia) — a rare compound; literally, watching with head stretched forward in tense expectation. The creation is not passive but actively straining toward the horizon — one of only two NT occurrences (also Phil 1:20). The object of the longing is τὴν ἀποκάλυψιν τῶν υἱῶν τοῦ θεοῦ (the revelation of the sons of God), linking vv. 14–17 to this cosmic frame.
8.20: ματαιότης (mataiotes) — futility, emptiness, vanity; the same word used throughout LXX Ecclesiastes for hebel (vapor/vanity) in post-fall existence. The creation was subjected οὐχ ἑκοῦσα (not willingly), marking this condition as alien to its created purpose. The subjection is ἐφ' ἑλπίδι (upon/in hope) — hope is a structural component of the subjection, not an added comfort.
8.21: δουλεία τῆς φθορᾶς (slavery of corruption/decay) — phthora covers both physical decay and moral corruption; the liberation promised is from both. The telos is ἐλευθερία τῆς δόξης τῶν τέκνων τοῦ θεοῦ — three stacked genitives compounding the richness of the eschatological promise.
8.22: συστενάζει and συνωδίνει — two syn- compound verbs: the whole creation groans together and is in labor-pain together ἄχρι τοῦ νῦν (until now). The birth-labor metaphor is not merely suffering but productive suffering pointing toward a definite end — the same image Paul uses for the labor pains of the final age in 1 Thess 5:3.
8.23: ἀπαρχὴν τοῦ πνεύματος — the firstfruits of the Spirit; the Spirit is possessed now as the initial installment of the full harvest to come (cf. arrabōn, down payment, in 2 Cor 1:22). υἱοθεσίαν reappears (cf. v. 15) but now as something still awaited — specifically τὴν ἀπολύτρωσιν τοῦ σώματος (the redemption of the body). Adoption is both now (v. 15) and not yet (v. 23); its bodily dimension remains outstanding.
8.24: τῇ ἐλπίδι ἐσώθημεν — we were saved in/by hope. The aorist passive marks salvation as an accomplished event, but hope is the modality in which that salvation is currently held. Hope that sees its object is no longer hope — ἐλπιζόμενον (what is hoped for) by definition lies beyond present sight.
8.25: δι' ὑπομονῆς (through patient endurance) — hypomonē is not passive resignation but active steadfastness under pressure; it recurs in Paul as the characteristic posture of those whose salvation is held eschatologically. The present ἀπεκδεχόμεθα (we are eagerly awaiting) picks up the cosmic waiting of vv. 19–23.
8.26: ὑπερεντυγχάνει (intercedes for us) — a NT hapax; the hyper- prefix intensifies the intercession: the Spirit intervenes with a force that transcends our own prayer capacity. στεναγμοῖς ἀλαλήτοις (unutterable/wordless groans) — alalētos means either "inexpressible" (groans one cannot articulate) or "unuttered" (the Spirit's groans are beyond human speech); the latter coheres better with the Spirit as the one interceding rather than the one through whom we speak.
8.27: ὁ ἐρευνῶν τὰς καρδίας (the one who searches the hearts) — a divine attribute from Jer 17:10 and Ps 139; here applied to the Father who knows the mind of the Spirit because the Spirit intercedes κατὰ θεόν (in accordance with God). The Spirit's intercession is therefore perfectly effective — never out of alignment with the divine purpose.
8.28: πάντα συνεργεῖ — all things work together. A significant variant in manuscript D and some patristic sources reads "God works all things together with those who love him," changing the subject from "all things" to "God." Either reading is grammatically possible; the context (God as active subject in vv. 29–30) slightly favors the variant. κατὰ πρόθεσιν (according to purpose) — prothesis means a prior-setting-forth, a deliberate plan; it anticipates the vocabulary of vv. 29–30.
8.29: προέγνω (foreknew) — the verb proginōskō in Pauline usage implies relational knowledge, not merely prior cognition. In OT idiom, to "know" someone is to enter covenant relation (cf. Amos 3:2); the pro- adds temporal priority. εἰκόνος τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ (the image of his Son) — the goal of predestination is conformity to Christ, recovering the imago Dei compromised at Adam (Gen 1:26). πρωτότοκον ἐν πολλοῖς ἀδελφοῖς — firstborn among many siblings: Christ is the eldest of a family, not an isolated figure.
8.30: The golden chain: προορίζω → καλέω → δικαιόω → δοξάζω (predestined → called → justified → glorified). All four verbs are aorist: glorification is stated alongside calling and justification as accomplished from the divine standpoint. The proleptic aorist treats the not-yet-accomplished glorification as so certain it can be narrated in the same past tense as the rest. The chain has no gaps.
8.31: The rhetorical unit (vv. 31–39) proceeds by a series of unanswerable questions. The opening εἰ ὁ θεὸς ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν (if God is for us) is not a conditional doubt but a conditional foundation — since God is for us, the questions that follow have no threatening answer. The argument moves from particular (God gave the Son) to universal (he will withhold no lesser gift).
8.32: τοῦ ἰδίου υἱοῦ οὐκ ἐφείσατο (did not spare his own Son) — the verb pheidomai (to spare) echoes LXX Gen 22:16, where God commends Abraham for not sparing his son Isaac. The verbal parallel is deliberate: the Father acts as Abraham acted — and goes further, actually delivering his Son where Abraham was stopped. The argument runs from greater to lesser: if he gave the greatest gift, no lesser gift will be withheld.
8.33: ἐγκαλέσει (will bring a charge) — the future of enkaleō, a forensic term for lodging a legal accusation. The answer is not "no one" but "God who justifies": the judge himself has already rendered a favorable verdict. A charge against the justified is self-defeating; the one before whom the charge would be brought is the one who declared them righteous.
8.34: τίς ὁ κατακρινῶν (who is the one who will condemn) — the future participle; the anticipated condemner is answered by four statements about Christ: he died, more than that he was raised, he is at God's right hand, and he intercedes for us. The final clause (ὃς καὶ ἐντυγχάνει ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν) places Christ's heavenly intercession as the present answer to every potential condemnation.
8.35: τίς ἡμᾶς χωρίσει ἀπὸ τῆς ἀγάπης τοῦ Χριστοῦ — seven pressures are listed: θλῖψις (trouble), στενοχωρία (hardship), διωγμός (persecution), λιμός (famine), γυμνότης (nakedness), κίνδυνος (danger), μάχαιρα (sword). Each was a material reality for Paul's readers, not a hypothetical; the list anchors the argument in the known experience of the Roman communities.
8.36: The Psalm 44:22 (LXX 43:23) quotation — ἕνεκέν σου θανατούμεθα ὅλην τὴν ἡμέραν (for your sake we are being killed all day long) — identifies the suffering of v. 35 with the covenant community's experience throughout Israel's history. Being counted as sheep for slaughter is not an anomaly but the scriptural pattern of the righteous under pressure; Paul cites it to prevent the community from reading suffering as evidence of divine abandonment.
8.37: ὑπερνικῶμεν (we more than conquer) — a NT hapax; the hyper- prefix intensifies ordinary conquest. The victory is not despite the sufferings of v. 35 but through them (ἐν τούτοις, in all these things) and not by self-strength but διὰ τοῦ ἀγαπήσαντος ἡμᾶς (through him who loved us).
8.38: The catalog of potential separators expands beyond personal suffering: θάνατος (death), ζωή (life), ἄγγελοι (angels), ἀρχαί (principalities), ἐνεστῶτα (things present), μέλλοντα (things to come), δυνάμεις (powers). The inclusion of "life" alongside "death" is notable: even the attachments and demands of life cannot sever the bond.
8.39: ὕψωμα and βάθος (height and depth) — likely astronomical terms for the zenith and nadir of celestial bodies in Paul's cosmological vocabulary. The final οὔτε τις κτίσις ἑτέρα (nor any other created thing) closes the catalog comprehensively: nothing within creation — no cosmic power, no temporal category, no spatial dimension — can accomplish the separation. The love of God is the one uncreated constant.

9.1: ἀλήθειαν λέγω ἐν Χριστῷ — three layers of attestation frame this claim: "I am telling the truth," "in Christ" (Paul's union with Christ as the ground of truthfulness), and "my conscience bearing witness in the Holy Spirit." The triple reinforcement signals Paul is anticipating incredulity; the emotional claim of vv. 2–3 would strain belief.
9.2: λύπη (grief) and ἀδιάλειπτος ὀδύνη (unceasing anguish) — two distinct terms. Odynē is the sharper word, used for the physical agony of wounds (cf. Luke 16:24–25); adialeipton (unceasing) marks it as chronic, not episodic. The accumulation establishes, before the difficult theological argument of chapter 9, that Paul's treatment of Israel's situation is not cool academic detachment.
9.3: ηὐχόμην — imperfect of eύchomαi, not aorist; the imperfect marks the wish as ongoing or as one recognizably impossible even as expressed: "I was wishing / I could wish." The wish to become ἀνάθεμα (accursed, under the ban, cut off from Christ) for the sake of kinspeople mirrors Moses in Exod 32:32 and demonstrates that Paul's theological argument does not produce indifference to ethnic Israel.
9.4: The catalog of Israel's privileges proceeds as predicate nominatives: υἱοθεσία (adoption/sonship), δόξα (the divine glory-presence), αἱ διαθῆκαι (the covenants — plural, acknowledging multiple covenant-making moments: Abraham, Sinai, David), ἡ νομοθεσία (the law-giving), ἡ λατρεία (the temple worship/service), αἱ ἐπαγγελίαι (the promises).
9.5: τὸ κατὰ σάρκα (according to the flesh) — the earthly genealogical descent of the Messiah within Israel. The clause that follows — ὁ ὢν ἐπὶ πάντων θεὸς εὐλογητὸς εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας (who is over all, God blessed forever) — is genuinely contested: is it an appositional description of Christ (an early NT confession of his deity) or a separate doxology to God the Father? Syntactically the natural reading links the clause to Christ as subject, but manuscript punctuation is uncertain. Both readings have sustained patristic and modern defenders.
9.6: οὐχ οἷον ὅτι ἐκπέπτωκεν (it is not as though [God's word] has fallen/failed) — the perfect tense ekpeptōken is important: the word of God has not yet fallen and remains standing. The assertion οὐ γὰρ πάντες οἱ ἐξ Ἰσραὴλ οὗτοι Ἰσραήλ distinguishes the ethnic community ("those from Israel") from the covenant community ("Israel"): physical descent does not constitute membership in the entity God was promising to.
9.7: ἐν Ἰσαὰκ κληθήσεταί σοι σπέρμα (in Isaac your seed shall be called) — the future passive klēthēsetai: the divine calling determines which line counts as the promised seed, not biological derivation. The quotation (Gen 21:12) shifts the burden of identification from genealogy to election by naming.
9.8: τέκνα τοῦ θεοῦ = τέκνα τῆς ἐπαγγελίας — the equation is exact: children of God and children of promise are coextensive. Physical descent produces physical children; the promise of God produces children of God. The logic runs through the Abraham argument of chapter 4.
9.9: κατὰ τὸν καιρὸν τοῦτον (at this appointed time) — kairos, not chronos: an appointed, significant time rather than mere clock-time. The Gen 18:10 promise depicts a God who operates according to a schedule — the promise of Isaac is a divinely-timed intervention, not a natural event.
9.10: The syntax emphasizes common origin: ἐξ ἑνός (from one man, Isaac). Unlike the Isaac/Ishmael case which could be attributed to different mothers, Esau and Jacob share both parents and the same conception. This eliminates maternal lineage and birth order as variables, leaving divine purpose as the only differentiating factor.
9.11: ἵνα ἡ κατ' ἐκλογὴν πρόθεσις τοῦ θεοῦ μένῃ — "so that God's purpose according to election might stand." Three terms compound: eklogē (election/selection), prothesis (deliberate prior purpose), and menē (subjunctive of μένω, to remain/stand). The purpose clause is explicit: the timing of the announcement (before birth, before works) is designed to establish that standing is contingent on God's prior purpose, not on human action.
9.12: ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι (the greater will serve the lesser) — the oracle reverses expected primogeniture; in the ANE world, the firstborn held the dominant social position. The passive ἐρρέθη (it was said) identifies the reversal as divinely spoken, not incidentally worked out.
9.13: τὸν Ἰακὼβ ἠγάπησα, τὸν δὲ Ἠσαῦ ἐμίσησα (Jacob I loved, Esau I hated) — from Mal 1:2–3. The Hebrew sānēʾ (hate) in Semitic idiom can function as a contrast meaning "love less" or "disregard" (cf. Luke 14:26); in Malachi it refers to the nations descended from Esau, not Esau's individual fate. Paul uses it not to address personal salvation but to illustrate that God's elective purpose operates without prior claim from the human side.
9.14: μὴ ἀδικία παρὰ τῷ θεῷ (is there unrighteousness with God?) — the negative particle mē expects a "no" answer. Paul formulates the objection directly (as in 3:5), then refuses to let the logic of election collapse into a charge of divine injustice. Mē genoito (by no means) is his strongest negative rhetorical marker.
9.15: The Exod 33:19 quotation — ἐλεήσω ὃν ἂν ἐλεῶ, καὶ οἰκτιρήσω ὃν ἂν οἰκτίρω (I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion) — is given to Moses at the moment Israel had broken the covenant with the golden calf. Mercy is exercised toward a people who had just forfeited any claim to it; the pattern of pure mercy is established at Israel's lowest covenant-keeping point.
9.16: ἄρα οὖν οὐ τοῦ θέλοντος οὐδὲ τοῦ τρέχοντος (not of the one who wills nor of the one who runs) — two present participles used as nouns representing human effort in its two modes: willing (internal resolve) and running (active exertion). The double negative is emphatic: neither contributes to the outcome. The single operative agent is τοῦ ἐλεῶντος θεοῦ (the God who shows mercy).
9.17: εἰς αὐτὸ τοῦτο ἐξήγειρά σε (for this very purpose I raised you up) — exēgeira means to raise up or bring onto the stage of history, not resurrection; the LXX uses it of raising up a king or leader. The divine use of Pharaoh's resistance serves two stated purposes: display of divine power and proclamation of the divine name in all the earth — global redemptive-historical purposes, not merely punitive ones.
9.18: ὃν θέλει σκληρύνει (he hardens whom he wills) — the assertion is unqualified here. The Exodus narrative shows a more complex process (Pharaoh first hardens himself; divine hardening follows in response), but Paul states the divine sovereignty side without qualification, letting the objection it raises drive the argument of vv. 19–21 forward. Sklērynō is the LXX Exodus term.
9.19: τί ἔτι μέμφεται (why does he still find fault) — memphomai = to censure; the questioner treats divine sovereignty as eliminating moral responsibility. τῷ βουλήματι αὐτοῦ (his will/purpose) — boulēma is the deliberate, determined resolve, stronger than mere inclination (thelēma).
9.20: ὦ ἄνθρωπε — the direct vocative intensifies the rebuke. μενοῦνγε (nay, rather!) — a strong adversative particle. Paul's response is not a philosophical solution to the problem of sovereignty but an appeal to creaturely limits: the formed thing does not interrogate the former. The potter-clay image from Isa 29:16 and Jer 18:6 frames what follows.
9.21: ἐξουσίαν (authority/right) — the potter's sovereign authority over the clay. The same word is used of Jesus's authority throughout the Gospels. The language of honor and dishonor (εἰς τιμήν / εἰς ἀτιμίαν) belongs to the Roman household familiar to Paul's audience — vessels for special use versus vessels for common use.
9.22: The sentence is grammatically incomplete — an anacoluthon: "What if God, willing to show wrath and make power known, bore with great patience the objects of wrath prepared for destruction..." The main clause never arrives; the reader must supply the conclusion. κατηρτισμένα (perfect passive participle, "prepared for destruction") — the passive voice leaves the agent ambiguous. The middle reading ("who fitted themselves") has defenders; the passive reading (God as agent) is more natural but is not resolved, and Paul does not resolve it.
9.23: πλοῦτος τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ (the riches of his glory) — the purpose clause identifies the positive side: vessels of mercy demonstrate the wealth of divine glory. Proētoimasen (prepared in advance) uses the same root as katērtismena in v. 22 but with a different preposition and direction; the advance preparation for glory frames the mercy.
9.24: οὓς καὶ ἐκάλεσεν (whom he also called) — these vessels of mercy are identified as drawn from both Jews and Gentiles. The preposition ἐξ (from/out of) in both phrases indicates that the called community is drawn from within the larger ethnic communities, not identified with them entirely.
9.25: Hosea 2:23 is applied to Gentiles: καλέσω τὸν οὐ λαόν μου λαόν μου (I will call the not-my-people my people). The original Hosea passage addresses apostate Israel, not Gentiles; Paul applies it to the broader principle that God calls into covenant status those who had none. The reversal of "not-my-people" to "my people" is accomplished by the divine speech act of calling, not by prior merit.
9.26: Hosea 1:10 continues: where the designation "not my people" was spoken, there the name "sons of the living God" will be given. The locative force (ἐκεῖ, there) marks a spatial coincidence between shame and restoration — the very site of rejection becomes the site of naming.
9.27: Isaiah 10:22–23 (LXX) introduces the remnant motif: though Israel's number is like the sand of the sea, only τὸ ὑπόλειμμα (the remnant) will be saved. This is not comfort for small numbers but a demonstration that within the large physical community, a smaller elect community is the operative concept — the same logic as the Isaac/Jacob arguments.
9.28: λόγον γὰρ συντελῶν καὶ συντέμνων (the Lord will accomplish his word, completing and cutting short) — the double participle emphasizes both completion and compression of the divine work; judgment is swift and decisive, not extended or partial.
9.29: Isaiah 1:9 is introduced as spoken by Isaiah προείρηκεν (has said beforehand) — the perfect tense gives the OT word ongoing relevance. κύριος σαβαώθ — Paul follows the LXX transliteration of YHWH Tzevaʾot (Lord of armies/hosts). The point is survival of a remnant by divine preservation alone; without that intervention, complete destruction would have followed.
9.30: The conclusion of the chapter's argument is stated as a paradox: Gentiles who did not pursue righteousness have attained it (κατέλαβεν, apprehended), specifically δικαιοσύνην δὲ τὴν ἐκ πίστεως (a righteousness from faith). Pursuit is not the mechanism — attainment comes by an unexpected route.
9.31: Israel, by contrast, pursued νόμον δικαιοσύνης (a law of righteousness) and did not arrive at the law. The failure is not in the goal (righteousness is the right goal) but in the mode: they pursued ὡς ἐξ ἔργων (as if from works) rather than receiving it from God through faith.
9.32: προσέκοψαν τῷ λίθῳ τοῦ προσκόμματος — they stumbled over the stone of stumbling. The composite quotation in v. 33 combines Isa 8:14 (a stone of stumbling and rock of offense) with Isa 28:16 (the precious cornerstone in Zion). Both Isaiah passages announce a divine purpose that confounds those who refuse to trust what God is doing.
9.33: The two Isaiah texts are woven together: ἰδοὺ τίθημι ἐν Σιὼν λίθον προσκόμματος from Isa 8:14, and ὁ πιστεύων ἐπ' αὐτῷ οὐ καταισχυνθήσεται (the one who believes on him will not be put to shame) from Isa 28:16. The conflation is an early Christian exegetical practice (cf. 1 Pet 2:6–8). Paul uses both texts as pointing to Christ: simultaneously cornerstone for those who believe and stumbling stone for those who do not.

10.1: εὐδοκία τῆς ἐμῆς καρδίας (the good pleasure/desire of my heart) — eudokia is an active, settled goodwill or longing, not mere wishing. The δέησις (supplication) is a specific request, more pointed than general prayer (proseuché). Together they frame Paul's pastoral orientation toward ethnic Israel before the theological argument resumes.
10.2: ζῆλον θεοῦ ἔχουσιν (they have zeal for God) — zēlos is acknowledged as genuine; Paul does not impugn Israel's sincerity. But οὐ κατ' ἐπίγνωσιν (not according to accurate knowledge) — epignōsis is the fuller, participatory knowledge of recognition; the problem is not quantity of information but the kind of knowing that comes from encounter with the reality itself.
10.3: τὴν ἰδίαν δικαιοσύνην (their own righteousness) vs. τῇ δικαιοσύνῃ τοῦ θεοῦ (the righteousness of God) — idian (own) marks the self-generated character of the first; the "righteousness of God" was established in chapters 1–4 as God's saving activity, not merely an attribute. Not submitting to God's righteousness means refusing to receive it as gift.
10.4: τέλος γὰρ νόμου Χριστός — "Christ is the telos of the law." The Greek word telos bears three overlapping senses: end (termination), goal (the purpose toward which one aims), and fulfillment (completion). All three contribute: Christ ends the law as a system of status-acquisition; he is the goal the law was pointing toward; he fulfills it in his obedience. The MKT's "culmination" captures the teleological dimension — the law was aimed at Christ — without foreclosing the other senses.
10.5: The Moses quotation from Lev 18:5 — ὁ ποιήσας αὐτὰ ἄνθρωπος ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς (the person who does these things will live by them) — describes the structure of law-righteousness: life follows from doing. Paul cites it accurately as a real description of that system, which is precisely why it falls short: no one can sustain the doing.
10.6: ἡ δὲ ἐκ πίστεως δικαιοσύνη οὕτως λέγει (the righteousness from faith speaks thus) — Paul personifies the principle and gives it voice. He applies Deut 30:12–14 to the gospel: the accessibility of the word of God (Moses' original point about Torah) becomes the accessibility of the word about Christ. The rhetorical move converts Deuteronomy's case for Torah nearness into evidence for gospel nearness.
10.7: The two negative questions in vv. 6–7 — "who will ascend into heaven" (to bring Christ down) and "who will descend into the abyss" (to bring Christ up from the dead) — reject the idea that the gospel requires extraordinary human achievement. The Incarnation and Resurrection are God's accomplished acts; they are not projects awaiting human initiation.
10.8: ἐγγύς σου τὸ ῥῆμά ἐστιν (the word is near you) — Paul transfers Deuteronomy's affirmation of Torah's nearness to the gospel: the rhēma tēs pisteōs (word of faith) that Paul preaches is equally near, equally in mouth and heart. The accessibility argument is identical; the referent has changed from Torah-obedience to faith-confession.
10.9: κύριον Ἰησοῦν (Lord Jesus) — the confession places kyrios first. Kyrios is the LXX rendering of YHWH; to call Jesus kyrios is to apply the divine name to him. The paired assertion of mouth (Lord Jesus) and belief of heart (God raised him from the dead) unites the two movements of saving response.
10.10: καρδίᾳ πιστεύεται εἰς δικαιοσύνην (with the heart one believes unto righteousness) and στόματι ὁμολογεῖται εἰς σωτηρίαν (with the mouth one confesses unto salvation) — the datives are instrumental. The structure does not imply two sequential stages (believe, then confess to get saved) but two aspects of the single act of faith, with slightly different telic phrases (dikaiosynē and sōtēria) that are themselves closely related.
10.11: The Isa 28:16 quotation reappears (cf. 9:33) with the addition of πᾶς (everyone): "everyone who believes on him will not be put to shame." The pas universalizes the promise beyond its original address and prepares the Jew/Gentile equivalence of v. 12.
10.12: οὐ γάρ ἐστιν διαστολή (there is no distinction) — the same assertion as 3:22–23, now applied to the positive side: no distinction in the availability of blessing. ὁ αὐτὸς κύριος πάντων (the same Lord of all) richly blesses all who call on him, completing the Joel quotation framework.
10.13: Joel 2:32 (LXX 3:5) — πᾶς ὃς ἂν ἐπικαλέσηται τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου σωθήσεται (everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved). In Joel, kyrios = YHWH; Paul applies it to Jesus, performing the same identification as in v. 9. Epikaleō (call on) is a technical term for invoking a deity — prayer and worship directed to the one named.
10.14: The chain of necessity runs in reverse order: calling requires believing, believing requires hearing, hearing requires a preacher. Each πῶς (how?) question tightens the logical sequence. The device is a sorites (chain argument), showing that the endpoint (calling on the Lord) is dependent on every link including the first (sending).
10.15: ἐὰν μὴ ἀποσταλῶσιν (unless they are sent) — aorist passive subjunctive; the sending is prior to all else, and it is passive: preachers are sent, not self-appointed. The Isa 52:7 quotation — ὡς ὡραῖοι οἱ πόδες τῶν εὐαγγελιζομένων (how beautiful the feet of those who bring good news) — roots the sending motif in the announcement of Israel's return from Babylonian exile.
10.16: οὐ πάντες ὑπήκουσαν τῷ εὐαγγελίῳ (not all obeyed the gospel) — hypakoúō combines akoúō (hearing) with hypo (under/submitting); not obeying the gospel is a failure of responsive hearing, not merely intellectual disagreement. The Isa 53:1 quotation (τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν, who has believed our report) shows this pattern of unresponsive hearing was anticipated in the servant song.
10.17: ἄρα ἡ πίστις ἐξ ἀκοῆς (faith comes from hearing/the message) — akoē is both the act of hearing and the message heard (cf. Gal 3:2, 5). The genitive ῥήματος Χριστοῦ (word of Christ) could be objective (the word about Christ) or subjective (the word that Christ speaks); both fit the context and both are likely intended.
10.18: μὴ οὐκ ἤκουσαν (did they not hear?) — the double negative expects a "yes, they did hear." The Ps 19:4 quotation — the voice of creation going out to all the earth — is applied to the proclamation of the gospel as evidence that the hearing opportunity has been universal. Paul treats the silent speech of the heavens typologically as applicable to the global reach of the gospel message.
10.19: μὴ Ἰσραὴλ οὐκ ἔγνω (did Israel not know?) — sharper than v. 18; not just "did they hear?" but "did they understand?" The Deut 32:21 quotation pairs with Isa 65:1–2 in vv. 19–21: Moses predicted Israel would be provoked to jealousy by a non-nation, and Isaiah proclaimed God was found by those not seeking him — the Gentile mission was embedded in Israel's own scriptures.
10.20: ἀποτολμᾷ (dares / speaks boldly) — a NT hapax; the apo- prefix intensifies the daring. Isaiah's boldness consists in depicting God as found by Gentiles who were not seeking him at all (ἐμφανὴς ἐγενόμην τοῖς ἐμὲ μὴ ζητοῦσιν, I was manifested to those not seeking me) — a reversal of normal covenant logic.
10.21: ὅλην τὴν ἡμέραν ἐξεπέτασα τὰς χεῖράς μου (all day long I have stretched out my hands) — the divine gesture of outstretched arms images persistent, patient invitation toward λαὸν ἀπειθοῦντα καὶ ἀντιλέγοντα (a disobedient and contradicting people). The Isa 65:2 quotation ends chapter 10 with both divine persistence (all day long) and human intransigence (disobedient, contradicting) held together, setting up chapter 11's opening question of whether God has therefore rejected his people.
